content.usage: With her talent and work ethic, she is definitely going places.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a person packed and moving forward, not standing still. They are literally Going (जा रहे) to better Places (जगहें) in life. It implies rapid Progress (तरक्की) and success.
extras.origin: American colloquialism from the early 20th century, referring to upward social or professional mobility.

meanings.english: To be in loss or debt.
meanings.hindi: घाटे में होना; कर्ज में होना।

content.usage: The company has been in the red for the last three quarters.

extras.mnemonic: In old account books, Profit was written in Black ink, and Loss (घाटा) was written in Red ink. Being 'in the red' means your account shows a Negative (नकारात्मक) balance.
extras.origin: From standard bookkeeping practices where red ink was used to denote debts or losses, contrasting with black ink for credits or profits.

meanings.english: To be abandoned in a difficult situation (often implying blame falls on them).
meanings.hindi: मुसीबत में अकेला छोड़ दिया जाना।

content.usage: When the partners fled with the money, he was left stranded to face the creditors.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a ship stuck on the Sand (रेत/तट) unable to move back to sea. The crew is Helpless (असहाय). To be left stranded means to be abandoned with no way out.
extras.origin: Nautical term. A 'strand' is a beach or shore. A ship that ran aground was 'stranded', leaving the crew stuck and helpless.

meanings.english: To go away; to leave.

content.usage: It's getting late, I must be off now.

extras.mnemonic: Like a light switch. When you turn it Off (बंद), the light goes away. When you 'are off', your presence ends and you Leave (जाना).
extras.origin: Shortened form of 'be off and away'. Common colloquial usage.

meanings.english: Broadcast over the radio or on TV.

content.usage: The interview will be on the air at 9 PM tonight.

extras.mnemonic: Radio and TV signals travel invisibly through the Air (हवा). If a program is 'on the air', it is actively flying through the atmosphere to your device.
extras.origin: Originated with the invention of radio broadcasting in the early 20th century, referring to the transmission of signals through the atmosphere.

meanings.english: To move quickly towards someone in a determined & threatening way; to exert pressure.
meanings.hindi: दबाव डालना; डराते हुए तेजी से पास आना।

content.usage: The opposing army began to bear down on the small village.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a giant Bear (भालू) using its weight to Press Down (दबाना) on you. Or a large ship sailing downwind fast to attack. It implies heavy Pressure (दबाव) or threatening approach.
extras.origin: Originally nautical. To 'bear down' meant to sail downwind towards another ship, often aggressively. It later generalized to exerting pressure or effort.

meanings.english: Produce positive results.

content.usage: Our hard work finally began to bear fruit when sales doubled.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Tree. You water it and protect it (work hard). Finally, it 'Bears Fruit' (फल देना). The fruit is the Result (परिणाम) of your labor.
extras.origin: Biblical and agricultural metaphor. Just as a healthy tree produces fruit, a successful plan or effort produces a tangible reward.

meanings.english: Suffer the worst part of something.

content.usage: The coastal cities had to bear the brunt of the tsunami.

extras.mnemonic: 'Brunt' rhymes with Burnt (जलना). If you are at the front of a fire, you 'bear the brunt'—you get the worst burns. It means facing the Main Force (मुख्य प्रभाव) of a disaster.
extras.origin: 14th Century. 'Brunt' meant a sharp blow or the onset of an attack. Soldiers in the front line bore the brunt (main force) of the enemy charge.

meanings.english: To win; to be victorious.

content.usage: In the debate competition, she will surely bear the palm.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A winner walking holding (bearing) a Palm leaf (ताड़ का पत्ता) as a trophy. In ancient times, the palm leaf was the symbol of Victory (विजय).
extras.origin: Roman custom. Victorious gladiators and athletes were awarded palm branches as symbols of triumph. To 'bear the palm' meant to carry the symbol of victory.

meanings.english: To put forth one's maximum effort; to think very hard.
meanings.hindi: दिमाग खपाना; बहुत अधिक प्रयास करना।

content.usage: I have been beating my brains out trying to solve this puzzle.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: You are hitting (beating) your head to force an idea out of your Brain (दिमाग). It signifies Extreme Mental Effort (कड़ी दिमागी मेहनत) to solve a problem.
extras.origin: A hyperbolic expression dating back to the mid-19th century, visualizing the act of violent self-punishment in an attempt to force mental productivity.

meanings.english: Dropped or stopped at an early stage.
meanings.hindi: शुरुआत में ही रोक देना (सिर उठाते ही कुचल देना)।

content.usage: The rebellion was nipped in the bud by the swift action of the army.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a flower Bud (कली). If you Nip (cut) it off now, it will never grow into a flower. Stopping a problem 'in the bud' means destroying it at the Start (शुरुआत) before it gets big.
extras.origin: Horticultural metaphor from the 17th century. Gardeners pinch off (nip) certain buds to encourage better growth elsewhere or prevent unwanted development.

meanings.english: Irrelevant; not accurate.

content.usage: Your argument is interesting but beside the mark regarding this case.

extras.mnemonic: Think of Archery. The 'Mark' is the Bullseye (निशाना). If your arrow lands 'beside' (next to) the mark, you Missed (चूकना). Your point is not accurate or Relevant (प्रासंगिक).
extras.origin: Derived from archery or target shooting. If a shot lands 'beside the mark', it has missed the target, metaphorically meaning a comment that misses the point.

meanings.english: Most useful innovation in a long time; an excellent person or thing.
meanings.hindi: एक बहुत ही बेहतरीन और उपयोगी आविष्कार।

content.usage: He thinks his new phone is the best thing since sliced bread.

extras.mnemonic: Before 1928, you had to slice bread yourself with a knife—it was messy. Pre-sliced bread was a Huge Convenience (बड़ी सुविधा). So, any great new invention is compared to that moment.
extras.origin: From a marketing slogan in 1928 celebrating Otto Rohwedder's bread-slicing machine. It was advertised as the greatest step forward in baking, eventually becoming a standard for comparing innovations.

meanings.english: Unreasonable or unacceptable; outside the bounds of civilized behavior.

content.usage: His rude comments at the funeral were completely beyond the pale.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Pale' was a wooden Fence (बाड़ा) marking the safe area. Going 'beyond the pale' meant going outside the safe, civilized zone into the Wild (जंगली) area. It means behavior that is Unacceptable (अस्वीकार्य).
extras.origin: Historical. The 'Pale' referred to the area of Ireland under English control (The Pale of Dublin). The land beyond this boundary was considered uncivilized and dangerous. Thus, going beyond the pale meant leaving civilization.

meanings.english: A lot of money.

content.usage: Lawyers in corporate firms usually make big bucks.

extras.mnemonic: 'Buck' is a slang term for Dollar (डॉलर). 'Big Bucks' simply means Big Money (बड़ी रकम).
extras.origin: American frontier origin. 'Buck' refers to a buckskin (male deer skin), which was used as a unit of trade before paper currency became widespread. A 'buck' eventually became slang for a dollar.

meanings.english: Speak angrily without any reason; to react aggressively.

content.usage: I only asked the time, you don't have to bite my head off!

extras.mnemonic: Imagine talking to a Monster. You ask a simple question, and it snaps its jaws and bites your Head (सिर) off. It describes someone reacting with excessive, sudden Anger (गुस्सा).
extras.origin: A hyperbolic expression from the mid-20th century, visualizing a reaction so verbally aggressive that it resembles a savage beast attacking and eating someone.

meanings.english: To force yourself to do something unpleasant; to endure pain with courage.
meanings.hindi: मजबूरी में कड़वा घूंट पीना; दर्द सहना।

content.usage: I hate going to the dentist, but I'll just have to bite the bullet and go.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A soldier being operated on without medicine. To stop screaming from Pain (दर्द), he clenches his teeth on a lead Bullet (गोली). It means enduring something painful because you have no choice.
extras.origin: Military medicine history. Before anesthesia, wounded soldiers were given a lead bullet to bite down on during surgery to help them cope with the agony and prevent them from biting their tongue.

meanings.english: Misfortune; bad luck.

content.usage: The black ox has trodden on his foot, and he has lost all his wealth.

extras.mnemonic: Black is often the color of doom. An Ox (बैल) is heavy. If a 'Black Ox' steps on you, you are crushed by Dark/Heavy Luck (दुर्भाग्य).
extras.origin: From the old proverb 'The black ox has trod on his foot', symbolizing old age or misfortune. In antiquity, black oxen were sometimes associated with sacrifices to the infernal deities, symbolizing bad omens.

meanings.english: To react very angrily; to lose one's temper.
meanings.hindi: आग बबूला हो जाना; बहुत गुस्सा होना।

content.usage: When he saw the scratched car, he was about to blow a fuse.

extras.mnemonic: Think of an electrical circuit. If there is too much power (anger), the Fuse (फ्यूज) explodes/blows to stop the system. When a person 'blows a fuse', their anger Overloads (अतिभार) their control.
extras.origin: Electrical metaphor from the 20th century. Just as an overloaded circuit causes a safety fuse to melt or blow, intense emotional pressure causes a person to lose their temper.

meanings.english: Be very angry; to lose one's temper explosively.

content.usage: My father will blow his top when he sees my report card.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a Volcano. When pressure builds, it Blows its Top (शिखर उड़ जाना) and lava erupts. A person 'blowing their top' is erupting with Anger (गुस्सा).
extras.origin: Mid-20th century. A mechanical metaphor possibly relating to steam engines (safety valves blowing) or volcanic eruptions, describing a sudden release of built-up pressure/anger.

meanings.english: Of noble birth; belonging to a royal or aristocratic family.

content.usage: Despite being blue-blooded, the prince lived a very simple life.

extras.mnemonic: Rich nobles stayed inside and had very pale skin, making their Blue Veins (नीली नसें) clearly visible. Workers were tanned, so veins weren't seen. Visible blue veins = Noble Blood (शाही खून).
extras.origin: Translated from the Spanish 'sangre azul'. Spanish aristocrats claimed pure ancestry (free from Moorish admixture), and their pale skin made their blue veins prominent compared to the darker-skinned commoners.

meanings.english: Sums up to; the essential part of a situation.

content.usage: The whole argument boils down to who is going to pay for the damages.

extras.mnemonic: Think of cooking a watery soup. If you Boil (उबालना) it for a long time, the water evaporates, and only the Essential Sauce (सार) remains at the bottom. The core truth is what remains.
extras.origin: Culinary/Chemistry metaphor. 'Boiling down' a liquid reduces its volume to leave only the concentrated essence or residue. Figuratively, it means reducing a complex issue to its most basic element.

meanings.english: Do or discover something new; innovate.
meanings.hindi: नई शुरुआत करना; कुछ नया खोजना।

content.usage: The scientists are hoping to break new ground in cancer research.

extras.mnemonic: Before building a new house or farm, you must Dig (Break) the Ground (जमीन) that has never been touched before. It means starting something completely New (नया) and innovative.
extras.origin: Agricultural and construction metaphor. Breaking ground is the first step in ploughing a new field or starting a building project. It signifies the start of a pioneering effort.

meanings.english: Watching all his actions closely; monitoring oppressively.
meanings.hindi: सिर पर सवार रहना; बहुत करीब से निगरानी करना।

content.usage: I can't work properly with the boss breathing down my neck all day.

extras.mnemonic: If someone stands right behind you, so Close (करीब) that you can feel their warm Breath on your Neck (गर्दन), it is uncomfortable. It implies intense, annoying Scrutiny (निगरानी).
extras.origin: A metaphor for close proximity. It evokes the image of a pursuer or supervisor standing so close behind a person that their breath can physically be felt.

meanings.english: To be successful; to earn a living.

content.usage: As the only earning member, he has to bring home the bacon.

extras.mnemonic: Bacon (meat) represents Food and Money. If you 'bring it home', you are feeding your family. It means Earning (कमाना) the salary needed to survive.
extras.origin: Often linked to the 'Dunmow Flitch' tradition (1104 AD) where a couple proving marital harmony won a side of bacon. Later, in boxing slang, 'bringing home the bacon' meant winning the prize money.

meanings.english: Made audience applaud enthusiastically.
meanings.hindi: वाहवाही लूटना; दर्शकों को मंत्रमुग्ध करना।

content.usage: Her stunning performance brought the house down.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine an audience clapping and cheering so Loudly (जोर से) that the vibrations make the theater building (House) Collapse (गिरना). It implies a massive, thunderous reaction.
extras.origin: Theatrical slang from the mid-18th century. 'The House' refers to the audience or the theater itself. The expression suggests applause so raucous it threatens the building's structural integrity.

meanings.english: To argue against; to compete with.

content.usage: He broke a lance with the professor over the interpretation of the poem.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Two knights riding horses at each other. They hit with such force that their wooden Spears (Lances) break. Breaking a lance means you have Fought (लड़ा) or competed with someone.
extras.origin: Medieval jousting terminology. Knights fought with lances; when they struck an opponent hard enough, the lance would shatter. Figuratively, it came to mean engaging in a dispute or debate.

meanings.english: To use bad grammar.

content.usage: In his nervous speech, he broke Priscian's head several times.

extras.mnemonic: Priscian was a strict Roman Grammarian (व्याकरण आचार्य). If you speak bad grammar, it's like hitting him on the Head (सिर). Visual: Imagine smashing a grammar book over a teacher's head every time you make a mistake.
extras.origin: Refers to Priscian (Priscianus Caesariensis), a renowned Latin grammarian of the 6th century. In the Middle Ages, violating the rules of grammar was metaphorically described as assaulting or injuring Priscian himself.

meanings.english: Support that failed; an unreliable person.

content.usage: I counted on his help, but he proved to be a broken reed.

extras.mnemonic: A Reed (सरकंडा) is a thin plant stem. If you try to use it as a Walking Stick (सहारा), it will Snap (टूट जाना). It represents a Support that fails when you lean on it.
extras.origin: Biblical origin (Isaiah 36:6). It compares trusting Egypt for help to leaning on a 'broken reed', which not only fails to support a man but pierces his hand.

meanings.english: Introduced for discussion; raised.
meanings.hindi: चर्चा के लिए प्रस्तुत करना; पालन-पोषण करना।

content.usage: The manager brought up the issue of salary increments during the meeting.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a topic lying at the bottom of a box. You 'Bring it Up' (ऊपर लाना) to the table so everyone can see and Discuss (चर्चा) it. Also applies to raising children (bringing them up to adulthood).
extras.origin: Standard phrasal verb usage. 'Up' implies movement from a lower state to a higher state of visibility (discussion) or maturity (child-rearing).

meanings.english: A mood of deep absorption or thoughtfulness; reverie.
meanings.hindi: गहन चिंतन; ख्यालों में खोया हुआ।

content.usage: He was sitting in a brown study and didn't hear the doorbell ring.

extras.mnemonic: Brown is a dull, dark color. 'Study' implies thinking. A 'Brown Study' is a state of mind that is Dark, Gloomy (उदास), or deeply absorbed in thought, oblivious to surroundings.
extras.origin: From the French word 'brun' meaning gloomy or dark. In medieval times, 'brown' meant gloomy. A 'brown study' originally meant a state of gloomy meditation, but now refers to deep absorption.

meanings.english: Irritates me; annoys me.

content.usage: It really bugs me when people chew with their mouths open.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Mosquito or Fly (कीड़ा/Bug) buzzing around your ears. It is annoying and Irritating (परेशान करने वाला). When someone 'bugs' you, they are acting like that annoying insect.
extras.origin: American slang from the mid-20th century. Derived from the annoyance caused by insects (bugs).

meanings.english: Do something that makes it impossible to return to the previous situation.
meanings.hindi: वापसी के रास्ते बंद कर देना।

content.usage: By resigning before finding a new job, he has burned his boats.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: An army lands on enemy shores. The commander Burns (जलाना) their own Boats. Now they cannot go back; they must Fight or Die. Burning boats means destroying your path of Return (वापसी).
extras.origin: Historical military tactic attributed to commanders like Alexander the Great, Tariq ibn Ziyad, and Hernán Cortés, who destroyed their own ships to ensure soldiers had no option of retreat, forcing them to conquer.

meanings.english: Interrupt; to interfere.

content.usage: Please don't butt in while I'm talking to my client.

extras.mnemonic: Goats fight by 'Butting' heads. Imagine you are talking, and a Goat (बकरी) suddenly Rams (घुसता है) its head between you and your friend. 'Butting in' is pushing yourself into a conversation rudely.
extras.origin: Derived from the behavior of horned animals (rams/goats) engaging in combat by butting heads. It metaphorically describes thrusting oneself into a situation or conversation.

meanings.english: By a small margin; barely.
meanings.hindi: बाल-बाल बचना; बहुत कम अंतर से।

content.usage: He won the race by a whisker.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Cat's Whisker (मूंछ का बाल). It is incredibly Thin (पतला). If you win a race 'by a whisker', you won by a distance as thin as a hair. It implies a very Close (करीबी) finish.
extras.origin: Originally a sporting term (horse racing or rowing), referring to winning by the smallest possible measurable distance—literally the width of a whisker.

meanings.english: Given or allowed by; thanks to the generosity of.

content.usage: The refreshments were provided by courtesy of the local bakery.

extras.mnemonic: Courtesy means Politeness (विनम्रता) or Generosity. If something is 'by courtesy of' Mr. X, it means Mr. X was kind enough to give it. It is thanks to his Favor (कृपा).
extras.origin: Derived from the feudal system where rights or lands were held 'by the courtesy' (favor/grace) of the Lord or King, rather than by legal right.

meanings.english: In the same way; for the same reason.

content.usage: You must respect your parents, and by the same token, they should respect your decisions.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Token' is a Symbol or Coin (सिक्का). If you use the 'same token', you are using the Same Logic (वही तर्क). It connects two ideas that follow the same rule or evidence.
extras.origin: 15th Century. 'Token' originally meant a sign, evidence, or proof. 'By the same token' meant 'the proof of this is also the proof of that', implying consistency in logic.

meanings.english: To scold; to reprimand.

content.usage: The teacher called down the student for being late.

extras.mnemonic: Think of God or a Master calling someone Down (नीचे) from their high horse to Scold (डांटना) them. Or calling down punishment from above. It implies humbling someone through reprimand.
extras.origin: Originally religious (calling down God's wrath/judgment). In domestic service, a master might 'call down' a servant to reprimand them. It evolved to mean scolding generally.

meanings.english: Decide or agree to stop doing something (usually work).

content.usage: We have been working for 10 hours; let's call it a day.

extras.mnemonic: You declare, 'This Day (दिन) is finished'. You are Marking the end (समाप्ति) of the working day. It means stopping work to go home and rest.
extras.origin: Originally 'call it half a day' (1838), used when leaving work early. By the 20th century, it was shortened to 'call it a day' to mean finishing work at the normal time or simply stopping for now.

meanings.english: Cancel something.

content.usage: They had to call off the match due to heavy rain.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a dog attacking. You shout 'Off!' to stop it. To 'Call Off' is to shout a command to Stop (रोकना) or Cancel (रद्द करना) an event or action.
extras.origin: Phrasal verb originating from hunting commands. A hunter would 'call off' his dogs to stop the pursuit. It generalized to mean canceling any planned event.

meanings.english: To be in control; to make the important decisions.
meanings.hindi: हुक्म चलाना; निर्णय लेने का अधिकार रखना।

content.usage: In this company, the marketing director calls the shots.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Imagine a Movie Director shouting 'Action!' or a Military Commander shouting 'Fire!'. The person who gives the orders to Shoot (गोली चलाना) is the one in Control (नियंत्रण). They are the boss.
extras.origin: Derived from target shooting training. A signalman would check the target and 'call' out where the shots hit to inform the marksman. In a broader sense, it refers to the person in charge of directing the action.

meanings.english: Became more sociable and confident.

content.usage: After joining the drama club, he really came out of his shell.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Tortoise (कछुआ). When it is scared/shy, it hides inside its Shell (ख खोल). When it feels Safe (सुरक्षित) and confident, it pokes its head out. A person 'coming out of their shell' stops being shy.
extras.origin: A natural history metaphor. Turtles, snails, and tortoises retract into their shells for protection when threatened. Emerging from the shell signifies dropping one's defenses and interacting with the world.

meanings.english: To be unable to do a job; failing to meet expectations.
meanings.hindi: उम्मीद पर खरा न उतरना; काम करने में असमर्थ होना।

content.usage: He was fired because he couldn't cut the mustard as a sales manager.

extras.mnemonic: Mustard plants are tough and stringy. To harvest (Cut/काटना) them by hand requires Strength (ताकत). If you are weak, you 'can't cut the mustard'. It means you aren't good enough for the task.
extras.origin: Late 19th-century American slang. 'Mustard' was used to describe something excellent or spicy (full of zest). Being able to 'cut' it meant being able to handle the best or hardest tasks.

meanings.english: In a respectful manner; humbly asking for something (usually money or forgiveness).
meanings.hindi: गिड़गिड़ाते हुए; बहुत विनम्रता से (अक्सर मदद मांगते समय)।

content.usage: He had to go cap in hand to his father to ask for a loan.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A beggar or a poor servant standing before a King. He takes off his Cap (टोपी) and holds it in his Hands (हाथ) as a sign of Respect (आदर). He is humbly asking for help or money.
extras.origin: Historical social etiquette. Inferiors (servants, peasants) were required to remove their hats when speaking to superiors. Keeping the hat in hand became the universal posture of humility and supplication.

meanings.english: Be in charge; to take responsibility.

content.usage: When the manager is away, the assistant has to carry the ball.

extras.mnemonic: Think of American Football or Rugby. The player who Carries the Ball (गेंद) is the one leading the attack. He is the most Important (महत्वपूर्ण) person at that moment, taking full Responsibility (जिम्मेदारी).
extras.origin: Sports metaphor from American Football. The player carrying the ball is the focal point of the play and is responsible for gaining ground for the team.

meanings.english: To take the responsibility (and often blame) for some misdemeanour.
meanings.hindi: किए का दोष अपने सिर लेना; बली का बकरा बनना।

content.usage: The CEO resigned, leaving the junior staff to carry the can for the mistake.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a Can (डिब्बा) filled with Explosives or something messy. If it blows up or spills, the person holding it gets Blamed (दोष). 'Carrying the can' means holding the burden when things go wrong.
extras.origin: Military slang. One theory suggests it refers to the private soldier sent to fetch a can of beer or rations for the mess; if anything went wrong (like spilling it), he was the one solely responsible and blamed.

meanings.english: Be important; to have influence.

content.usage: His opinion carries a lot of weight in the committee.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine two rocks. A heavy rock makes a deep Dent (निशान) in the ground. A light rock does nothing. If your words 'carry weight', they are Heavy/Important (महत्वपूर्ण) enough to leave an impact.
extras.origin: A physical metaphor equating mass with importance. Just as a heavy object exerts more force, a person or argument with 'weight' exerts more influence.

meanings.english: Developed a specific position for oneself; to create a unique role.

content.usage: She carved out a niche for herself in the competitive fashion industry.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A sculptor chipping away at a stone wall to make a Niche (आला/खांचा) for a statue. By working hard (Carving/तराशना), you create a special Space (जगह) that fits only you.
extras.origin: Architectural term. A 'niche' is a recess in a wall designed to hold a specific statue. Figuratively, it implies creating a specialized role or market segment that one fits into perfectly.

meanings.english: Damaged reputation; to speak badly of.

content.usage: The scandal cast a slur upon his previously spotless record.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Slur' originally meant thin Mud (कीचड़). If you Cast (throw) mud on someone's white shirt, it leaves a stain. Casting a slur means staining someone's Reputation (इज्जत).
extras.origin: From Middle English 'sloer' meaning mud or mire. Literally throwing mud at someone evolved metaphorically into smearing their reputation with insults or false accusations.

meanings.english: Offering good things to undeserving people who cannot appreciate them.
meanings.hindi: भैंस के आगे बीन बजाना (अयोग्य व्यक्ति को मूल्यवान वस्तु देना)।

content.usage: Explaining philosophy to him is like casting pearls before swine.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine throwing expensive Pearls (मोती) to Pigs/Swine (सूअर). The pigs can't eat them, so they just trample them in the mud. It is a Waste (बर्बादी) of value on those who don't understand it.
extras.origin: Biblical origin (Matthew 7:6): 'Give not that which is holy unto the dogs, neither cast ye your pearls before swine, lest they trample them under their feet'.

meanings.english: A particular situation in which one cannot do anything; a dilemma with no escape.
meanings.hindi: विकट स्थिति; धर्मसंकट (इधर कुआँ उधर खाई)।

content.usage: I need experience to get a job, but I need a job to get experience—it's a Catch 22.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a Law Book with Rule #22. It says: 'To get out of prison, you must be crazy. But if you ask to leave, you are sane, so you must stay.' It is a Trap (जाल) of logic with No Escape (कोई रास्ता नहीं).
extras.origin: Title of Joseph Heller's 1961 novel 'Catch-22'. In the book, pilots could only be grounded if they were crazy, but applying to be grounded proved they were sane (because fearing death is sane), so they had to keep flying.

meanings.english: To deal with a person who is more than one's match; to encounter a unexpectedly strong opponent.
meanings.hindi: शेर को सवा शेर मिलना; अपने से अधिक शक्तिशाली व्यक्ति से पाला पड़ना।

content.usage: The thief thought the old lady was an easy target, but he caught a tartar—she was a karate expert.

extras.mnemonic: The Tartars were fierce Mongolian Warriors (योद्धा). Imagine a soldier thinking he caught a weak prisoner, but the prisoner turns out to be a deadly Tartar who beats him up. He bit off more than he could chew.
extras.origin: 17th Century. Relates to a story of a soldier calling out that he had caught a Tartar (a feared Turkic/Mongol warrior). When told to bring him in, the soldier replied, 'He won't let me'. The captive was actually holding the captor.

meanings.english: Seize opportunity without delay.

content.usage: If you want to succeed, you must catch time by the forelock.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Father Time is an old man running past you. He is bald at the back but has a lock of Hair (Forelock/लट) in the front. You must grab him from the Front (सामने) as he approaches, or you miss him forever.
extras.origin: Ancient Greek mythology. Kairos (Opportunity) was depicted as having a long lock of hair on his forehead but being bald in the back, symbolizing that opportunity must be grasped as it approaches, for once it passes, it cannot be caught.

meanings.english: To be highly impressive; excellent.

content.usage: She thinks she's the cat's whiskers in that new dress.

extras.mnemonic: Cats are graceful and elegant. Their Whiskers (मूंछें) are fine, sensitive, and perfect. In the 1920s, people used nonsense animal parts (like 'bee's knees') to describe things that were Excellent (बेहतरीन).
extras.origin: 1920s slang (The Jazz Age). Coined during a fad for using animal anatomy to describe excellence (e.g., 'the cat's pajamas', 'the bee's knees'). It implies something stylish and superior.

meanings.english: Very close together; side by side.

content.usage: The poor and the rich lived cheek by jowl in the crowded city.

extras.mnemonic: Your Cheek (गाल) and your Jowl (Jaw/जबड़ा) are right next to each other on your face. They are Inseparable (जुड़े हुए). Being 'cheek by jowl' means standing extremely Close (पास).
extras.origin: First used by Shakespeare in 'A Midsummer Night's Dream' (1595). It vividly describes intimacy or crowding by referencing facial anatomy.

meanings.english: Discuss or consider something at length.

content.usage: I need a few days to chew over your proposal before deciding.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Cow. It Chews (चबाना) its food slowly for a long time to digest it properly. Similarly, you 'chew over' an Idea (विचार) in your mind to understand it fully before deciding.
extras.origin: A metaphor for digestion. Just as food needs thorough chewing to be digested, complex information needs to be mentally 'chewed' to be processed and understood.

meanings.english: Very little money; a negligible amount.

content.usage: He refused the job offer because the salary was chicken feed.

extras.mnemonic: What do chickens eat? Grain and corn. It is Cheap (सस्ता) and small. Comparing a salary to 'chicken feed' means it is so small it is barely enough to survive, like food for a bird.
extras.origin: American slang (early 20th century). Farm metaphor contrasting the low cost of grain used to feed poultry with substantial human food or wages.

meanings.english: To decide not to do something because you are too frightened.

content.usage: He was going to jump from the diving board, but he chickened out at the last minute.

extras.mnemonic: Chickens are easily Scared (डरपोक) and run away from noise. If you act like a Chicken, you run away from a challenge due to Fear (डर).
extras.origin: Since the 17th century, 'chicken' has been slang for cowardly. 'Chicken out' emerged in the mid-20th century (possibly WWII) to describe withdrawing from a task due to fear.

meanings.english: A weakness that can be taken advantage of; a vulnerability.

content.usage: The lawyer found chinks in the armour of the prosecution's case.

extras.mnemonic: A Knight wears a metal suit (Armour). A 'Chink' is a small Gap or Crack (दरार). If there is a gap, an arrow can get through and kill him. It represents a Fatal Weakness (कमजोरी) in a defense.
extras.origin: Medieval warfare. Plate armor was designed to be impenetrable, but the joints (chinks) were vulnerable spots where a blade or arrow could pierce through.

meanings.english: An activity that involves mystery and secrecy (usually espionage).

content.usage: I am tired of all this cloak and dagger stuff; just tell me the truth.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A Spy or Assassin wearing a dark Cloak (लबादा) to hide his face and holding a Dagger (खंजर) to attack secretly. This imagery represents Spying (जासूसी) and secrecy.
extras.origin: From the French 'de cape et d'épée' (of cloak and sword), referring to a genre of drama involving intrigue, spies, and secret fights. The term was adopted in English to describe espionage.

meanings.english: The evil intention; sign of the devil.

content.usage: Though he spoke sweetly, he eventually showed the cloven hoof.

extras.mnemonic: In folklore, the Devil (शैतान) can disguise himself as a human, but he cannot hide his feet, which are Hooves (खुर) like a goat's. Seeing the 'cloven hoof' means realizing someone is actually Evil (दुष्ट).
extras.origin: Biblical/Folklore. Satan is traditionally depicted with the split (cloven) hooves of a goat (Pan-like imagery). Showing the cloven hoof reveals one's true, demonic nature beneath a disguise.

meanings.english: Slight satisfaction; something that offers very little consolation.
meanings.hindi: झूठी तसल्ली; ना के बराबर राहत।

content.usage: The apology was cold comfort to the victims who lost their savings.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine you are shivering in the snow and ask for warmth. Someone hands you a block of Ice (बर्फ). That is 'Cold Comfort'—it provides No Relief (कोई राहत नहीं) at all.
extras.origin: First used in Shakespeare's 'King John'. It oxymoronically combines 'comfort' (warmth/solace) with 'cold', signifying consolation that is so slight it is practically useless.

meanings.english: No matter what happens; in spite of any obstacles.
meanings.hindi: चाहे कुछ भी हो जाए (आंधी आए या तूफान)।

content.usage: I will finish this marathon come hell or high water.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: I will reach my goal even if Fire (Hell/नर्क) burns me or Flood (High Water/बाढ़) blocks me. It implies Unstoppable Determination (दृढ़ संकल्प) regardless of any disaster.
extras.origin: American origin (19th century). Possibly refers to the difficulties of cattle herding—crossing scorching deserts (hell) and flooded rivers (high water). It signifies enduring extreme conditions.

meanings.english: Be separated; to succeed or happen as planned.

content.usage: The button came off my shirt. / The plan didn't come off as expected.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Button. If it 'Comes Off' (निकलना), it separates. Also, think of a Plan. If a plan 'Comes Off', it happens or Succeeds (सफल होना), like a stage performance going off well.
extras.origin: Standard phrasal verb. 'Off' suggests separation (button coming off) or completion/execution (an event coming off).

meanings.english: Get into a fight physically.
meanings.hindi: हाथापाई पर उतर आना; मारपीट करना।

content.usage: The argument escalated until they almost came to blows.

extras.mnemonic: When words fail, people start hitting. 'Blows' refers to Punches (मुक्का). Coming to blows means transitioning from arguing to Physical Fighting (मारपीट).
extras.origin: From the Middle English 'blaw', referring to a hit or stroke. To 'come to' implies reaching the stage of exchanging physical strikes.

meanings.english: Have an accident; meet with disaster or failure.

content.usage: His speeding car came to grief on the sharp turn.

extras.mnemonic: Grief usually means Sadness (दुख). If an endeavor 'comes to grief', it ends in a sad result, like an Accident (दुर्घटना) or failure.
extras.origin: Old English. Grief originally meant hardship or suffering. To 'come to grief' meant to meet with a mishap or unfortunate end.

meanings.english: To gradually accept a sad or difficult situation.
meanings.hindi: समझौता करना; स्थिति को स्वीकार कर लेना।

content.usage: It took him a year to come to terms with the loss of his job.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine signing a Contract. You agree to the Terms (शर्तें). 'Coming to terms' with a tragedy means you mentally 'sign the contract' to Accept (स्वीकारना) the reality, no matter how harsh.
extras.origin: Legal/Business metaphor. Originally meant reaching an agreement or settlement after a dispute. It evolved to describe the internal psychological process of accepting a difficult reality.

meanings.english: To falsify financial records; to cheat in accounts.

content.usage: The accountant was arrested for cooking the books to hide the company's losses.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a chef taking an Account Book (बही-खाता) and 'Cooking' it in a pot, adding spice to change the flavor. It means altering the Numbers (आंकड़े) to make them look better or to steal money.
extras.origin: 17th Century. The term 'cook' was slang for 'doctoring' or altering something. 'Cooking the books' specifically referred to manipulating financial ledgers dishonesty.

meanings.english: Ready to work; willing and relaxed regarding a task.
meanings.hindi: काम के लिए तैयार (बिना किसी नखरे के)।

content.usage: He is cool about working on weekends if the pay is good.

extras.mnemonic: Cool means Relaxed/Okay (शांत). If you are 'cool about' something, you are fine with it. Being 'cool about working' means you are Willing (तैयार) to work without complaining.
extras.origin: Modern colloquialism. 'Cool' in the sense of 'amenable' or 'agreeable' became popular in 20th-century slang.

meanings.english: To be kept waiting (often deliberately).

content.usage: The receptionist made him cool his heels in the lobby for an hour.

extras.mnemonic: When a horse runs, its hooves/heels get Hot (गर्म). When it stops to Wait (इंतजार), they cool down. Being forced to 'cool your heels' means being forced to wait and do nothing.
extras.origin: 17th Century. Originally referred to horses resting after a journey, allowing their hot hooves to cool. Later applied to people forced to wait.

meanings.english: Isolated; restricted access using a barrier.

content.usage: The police cordoned off the crime scene to preserve evidence.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Cordon' is a line of police or a Rope (रस्सी). Cordoning off means wrapping a rope around an area to Isolate (अलग करना) it so no one can enter.
extras.origin: From the French 'cordon' (ribbon or cord). Militarily, it referred to a line of troops enclosing an area. Now commonly refers to police tape or barriers.

meanings.english: Eliminate; to draw a line through.

content.usage: Please cross out the wrong answer and write the correct one.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Drawing a big 'X' or Cross (कांटा) over a word. It signals 'Delete' or 'Remove' (हटाना).
extras.origin: Standard writing instruction. The action of drawing a cross (X) through text to invalidate it.

meanings.english: To depreciate; to criticize or belittle.

content.usage: He always tries to cry down the achievements of his rivals.

extras.mnemonic: Cry means to Shout (चिल्लाना). Imagine shouting 'Down! Down!' at someone to lower their value. To 'cry down' is to Belittle (निंदा करना) or disparage someone publicly.
extras.origin: 17th Century. Opposite of 'cry up' (to praise). It comes from the custom of public announcements (town criers) decrying or depreciating the value of coinage or goods.

meanings.english: Already decided; clear and definite; routine.

content.usage: The solution is not cut and dried; we need to discuss it further.

extras.mnemonic: Think of herbs or timber sold in shops. If they are already Cut (कटा हुआ) and Dried (सूखा), they are Ready to use. No work is needed. It implies a plan that is settled and Fixed (तय).
extras.origin: Originally referred to herbs sold by herbalists. 'Cut and dried' herbs were ready for immediate use, unlike fresh herbs. The phrase came to mean anything ready-made, pre-arranged, or lacking freshness/originality.

meanings.english: Interrupt someone while they are speaking.

content.usage: It is rude to cut in when two people are having a serious conversation.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A pair of Scissors cutting a string. If someone is speaking a long sentence (string) and you 'Cut In', you sever their flow. You Interrupt (टोकना) them mid-sentence.
extras.origin: Metaphor of severing a continuous line or flow. Used in telecommunications and social etiquette to describe breaking into a connection or conversation.

meanings.english: To perform well; to meet the required standard.
meanings.hindi: सफल होना; उम्मीद पर खरा उतरना।

content.usage: He is too old to play football; he just can't cut the mustard anymore.

extras.mnemonic: Positive version of 'Can't cut the mustard' (See ID 1249). Cutting tough mustard stalks required a strong worker. If you can 'cut the mustard', you are Capable (सक्षम) and good enough for the job.
extras.origin: See ID 1249. Derived from the agricultural difficulty of harvesting mustard or the slang use of 'mustard' to mean 'excellent/zest'.

meanings.english: Complete failure; something that falls short of expectations.
meanings.hindi: ऊंची दुकान फीका पकवान; पूर्ण विफलता।

content.usage: The much-hyped launch of the product was a damp squib.

extras.mnemonic: Repeat of ID 1151. A wet Firework (पटाखा) that fails to explode. It signifies a Disappointing Failure (निराशाजनक विफलता) after high expectations.
extras.origin: Repeat of ID 1151. Mid-19th Century mining/fireworks terminology.

meanings.english: To die while in service; to die while still working before retirement.

content.usage: The prime minister died in harness after serving the country for 20 years.

extras.mnemonic: A Harness (साज़) is the gear a horse wears to pull a cart. If a horse 'dies in harness', it dies while working in the field, not resting in the stable. It means dying while still Employed (नौकरी में).
extras.origin: Agricultural metaphor. Working animals (horses/oxen) wore a harness. Dying in harness meant dying while actively working, applied metaphorically to humans who work until death.

meanings.english: Without money; destitute and defeated.

content.usage: After losing his business, he was down and out on the streets.

extras.mnemonic: Boxing metaphor. If you are knocked Down (नीचे) and the ref counts you Out (बाहर), the fight is over. You have Lost everything. Being 'down and out' in life means being Broke (कंगाल) and defeated.
extras.origin: Boxing terminology. A fighter who is knocked down and cannot rise before the count of ten is 'down and out'. Extended to describe anyone destitute or defeated in life.

meanings.english: Give a scolding; a severe reprimand.

content.usage: The boss gave him a dressing down for missing the deadline.

extras.mnemonic: Not about clothes! Think of 'dressing' wood or stone—hitting it to smooth it out. A 'Dressing Down' is a verbal Beating (पिटाई/डांट) to correct someone's behavior.
extras.origin: Likely from craftsmanship (dressing stone/wood) or 19th-century naval slang, where 'dressing' meant a thrashing or beating. It evolved to mean a severe verbal reprimand.

meanings.english: To emphasize an important point; to make something clearly understood.

content.usage: The teacher used examples to drive home the importance of honesty.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Hammer driving a Nail. You hit it repeatedly until it goes all the way 'Home' (into the wood). To 'drive home' a point is to Explain (समझाना) it forcefully until it sticks in the mind.
extras.origin: Mechanical/Carpentry metaphor. Driving a nail or bolt 'home' means securing it fully into its proper place. Figuratively, it means ensuring an argument is fully understood.

meanings.english: To encourage; to urge someone to do something (often foolish).

content.usage: His friends egged him on to jump over the fence.

extras.mnemonic: Nothing to do with chicken eggs! It comes from 'Edge' (किनारा). Visual: Pushing someone to the Edge of a cliff, urging them to jump. 'Egged on' means Pushing/Urging (उकसाना) someone to act.
extras.origin: From the Old Norse word 'eggja', meaning to edge or incite. It sounded like 'egg' to English speakers, leading to the modern phrase. It implies incitement or provocation.

meanings.english: A deception; nonsense; something done only for show.

content.usage: The committee's inquiry was just eyewash; they had no intention of punishing anyone.

extras.mnemonic: Eye wash (lotion) makes tired eyes feel good but doesn't cure blindness. It blurs vision temporarily. 'Eyewash' refers to Deceptive (धोखेबाज) words or actions meant to soothe or blind people to the truth.
extras.origin: 19th Century slang. Originally referred to a soothing lotion for the eyes. It came to mean 'flattery' or 'humbug'—something that looks good on the surface but obscures the reality.

meanings.english: To bear the consequences; to accept punishment.
meanings.hindi: किए का फल भुगतना; परिणाम का सामना करना।

content.usage: You broke the window, now you have to face the music.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: An actor walking on stage. He must Face the Orchestra (Music/संगीत) and the audience, even if he is nervous or made a mistake. He cannot hide. He must accept the Judgment (परिणाम).
extras.origin: Disputed origin. Could refer to an actor facing the orchestra pit, or a disgraced soldier being 'drummed out' of the regiment (facing the drums/music). It implies facing the consequences publicly.

meanings.english: To seek support out of necessity; to use an alternative plan.
meanings.hindi: सहारे के लिए उपयोग करना; विकल्प के रूप में अपनाना।

content.usage: If I lose my job, I have my savings to fall back on.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: You are pushed backwards. If there is a Cushion behind you, you 'Fall Back On' it safely. Your savings or skills are that Cushion (सहारा) that catches you when plans fail.
extras.origin: Military tactic. An army that is losing ground 'falls back' to a secondary defensive line for support. Metaphorically, relying on a reserve option.

meanings.english: Falling deeply in love with someone.
meanings.hindi: प्यार में पूरी तरह पागल होना।

content.usage: He fell head over heels for his new neighbor.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine tripping and rolling down a hill like a ball—Head over Heels (सिर के बल). You have lost Balance (संतुलन) and control. Falling in love feels just like that uncontrollable tumble.
extras.origin: Originally 'heels over head' (14th century), describing a somersault. It was corrupted to 'head over heels' in the 18th century. Used to describe a literal tumble, then metaphorically applied to falling in love.

meanings.english: An episode of frantic competition; aggressive attack by a group.

content.usage: The sale at the mall triggered a feeding frenzy among shoppers.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A pack of hungry Sharks attacking prey. They bite anything that moves in a Frenzy (पागलपन). A 'feeding frenzy' describes people (like shoppers or reporters) attacking or competing Aggressively (आक्रामक रूप से).
extras.origin: Marine biology term. Describes the chaotic behavior of predators (like sharks/piranhas) swarming over prey. It became a metaphor for intense, aggressive competition in journalism and commerce.

meanings.english: To begin to understand the work; to feel confident in a new situation.

content.usage: She is still finding her feet in the new job, give her some time.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Baby learning to walk. It wobbles at first, but soon it 'Finds its Feet' (संतुलन बनाना) and stands confidently. It means gaining Confidence (आत्मविश्वास) in a new situation.
extras.origin: Developmental metaphor referring to an infant or young animal learning to stand and walk. Applied to adults adjusting to new environments.

meanings.english: A market selling second-hand goods.
meanings.hindi: कबाड़ी बाजार; पुराने सामान का बाजार।

content.usage: I bought this antique lamp at a flea market for very cheap.

extras.mnemonic: Old clothes and furniture sold in cheap markets might have Fleas (पिस्सू/कीड़े). A 'Flea Market' sells Used (पुराना) stuff, implying it might be a bit dirty or cheap.
extras.origin: Translation of the French 'Marché aux puces' (Market of the fleas). This Parisian market sold second-hand goods that were rumored to be infested with fleas.

meanings.english: Puzzled; to shock or defeat someone completely.

content.usage: I was floored by the news of his sudden resignation.

extras.mnemonic: Boxing metaphor. A powerful punch knocks the fighter to the Floor (फर्श). Being 'floored' by news means you are so Shocked (हैरान) that you feel knocked down and speechless.
extras.origin: From boxing/fighting. To 'floor' someone is to knock them down. Figuratively, it means to defeat an argument or shock someone so much they are momentarily incapacitated.

meanings.english: To start discussing something irrelevant; to digress suddenly.

content.usage: The professor tends to fly off at a tangent during lectures, talking about his cats instead of physics.

extras.mnemonic: Think of Geometry. A Tangent is a line that touches a circle and then flies away in a Straight Line (सीधी रेखा) away from the center. Flying off at a tangent means leaving the Main Topic (मुख्य मुद्दा) and going somewhere irrelevant.
extras.origin: Geometric metaphor. A tangent line diverges from a curve. In conversation, it describes a train of thought that diverges abruptly from the main subject.

meanings.english: Suddenly becoming enraged; losing control of one's temper.
meanings.hindi: आपा खो बैठना; अचानक गुस्सा हो जाना।

content.usage: He is known for flying off the handle at the slightest provocation.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: You are swinging an Axe. Suddenly, the sharp Metal Head flies off the Wooden Handle (हत्था). It is dangerous and uncontrolled. A person 'flying off the handle' has lost Control (नियंत्रण) of their temper.
extras.origin: 19th-century American colloquialism. Refers to the head of a tool (like an axe or hammer) coming loose and flying off the handle while being swung, creating a sudden, dangerous situation.

meanings.english: Permanently; forever.

content.usage: She is leaving the country for good.

extras.mnemonic: Think of 'Good' as in 'Good and Done' (पूरी तरह से). If you leave 'for good', you leave for the final, valid time. It means Forever (हमेशा).
extras.origin: Short for the older phrase 'for good and all' (16th century), meaning finally and irrevocably.

meanings.english: A very old-fashioned person; someone fussy and critical.

content.usage: Don't be such a fuddy-duddy; let's go out and have some fun!

extras.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Muddy'. A 'Fuddy-Duddy' is stuck in the Mud of the past. They are Boring (बोरिंग), old-fashioned, and don't like fun or change.
extras.origin: Early 20th-century American slang. Likely a rhyming reduplication possibly derived from 'fussy', representing an old-fashioned, pompous person.

meanings.english: Full of nonsense; talking a lot without saying anything important.

content.usage: Don't believe his promises; he is just full of hot air.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Balloon. It is big and round, but inside it is only Hot Air (गर्म हवा). It has no substance. A person 'full of hot air' talks big but is Empty (खोखला) inside.
extras.origin: Refers to empty speech. It may relate to the fact that hot air rises (is light/insubstantial) or simply that speech is expelled breath (air).

meanings.english: Hateful; extremely bitter and unpleasant.

content.usage: The memory of that defeat is still gall and wormwood to him.

extras.mnemonic: Gall implies Bile (पित्त/कड़वा). Wormwood is a bitter Plant. Mixing two incredibly Bitter (कड़वा) things creates extreme unpleasantness or hatred.
extras.origin: Biblical reference (Lamentations 3:19): 'Remembering mine affliction and my misery, the wormwood and the gall.' It symbolizes extreme bitterness and suffering.

meanings.english: To express a negative emotion in a forceful way.

content.usage: He gave vent to his frustration by shouting at his colleagues.

extras.mnemonic: A Vent (निकास) is a hole that lets steam or smoke escape. If you bottle up your anger, you might explode. 'Giving vent' allows the Anger (गुस्सा) to escape forcefully, like steam.
extras.origin: Physics/Engineering metaphor. Confined gases or fluids need a vent to escape. Figuratively applied to emotions (anger, grief) that need release.

meanings.english: To start doing something quickly.

content.usage: We have a lot of work to do, so let's get cracking.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Whip cracking (कोड़ा फटकारना) to make a horse run fast. 'Get cracking' means to start moving Immediately (फटाफट) and work hard.
extras.origin: 20th-century slang. Probably refers to the sound of a whip cracking to urge action or speed.

meanings.english: To start discussing the important facts or practical details.

content.usage: Let's stop the small talk and get down to brass tacks about the budget.

extras.mnemonic: In old cloth shops, Brass Tacks (पीतल की कीलें) were nailed to the counter to measure exact yards of fabric. Getting down to the 'brass tacks' meant stopping the talk and doing the Real Measuring (असली काम).
extras.origin: American origin (19th century). Likely from the drapery trade, where brass tacks on the counter were used for measuring, signifying getting down to the precise business.

meanings.english: To be in trouble; to make things difficult.

content.usage: He got into a soup by lying to the police.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine falling into a pot of boiling Soup (सूप). It is hot, sticky, and you are being cooked. Getting 'into a soup' implies landing in a Hot/Messy Situation (मुसीबत).
extras.origin: Late 19th-century slang. Similar to 'in hot water'. It describes being in a difficult or messy predicament.

meanings.english: To irritate someone; to annoy.

content.usage: It really gets my goat when people cut in line.

extras.mnemonic: Racehorses often had a Goat (बकरी) friend to keep them calm. Rivals would Steal (Get) the goat to upset the horse before the race. Getting someone's goat means Annoying (चिढ़ाना) them.
extras.origin: Horse racing folklore. Goats were kept in stables to calm high-strung racehorses. Removing ('getting') the goat would agitate the horse, causing it to lose the race.

meanings.english: Learn how to use; to understand the technique of doing something.

content.usage: Driving is difficult at first, but you'll soon get the hang of it.

extras.mnemonic: Think of handling a heavy Tool like an Axe or Scythe. You need to know how it 'Hangs' (balance/वजन) in your hand to swing it properly. Getting the hang of it means mastering the Technique (तकनीक).
extras.origin: 19th Century. Likely refers to getting familiar with the 'hang' (balance/feel) of a tool, such as a scythe or cricket bat, to use it effectively.

meanings.english: To be dismissed from a job.

content.usage: He was lazy and eventually got the sack.

extras.mnemonic: In old times, workers carried their tools in a Sack (बोरी). When they were Fired (नौकरी से निकालना), the boss gave them their sack back to pack up and leave. Getting the sack = Losing the job.
extras.origin: Traceable to the French phrase 'on lui a donné son sac' (he was given his sack). Mechanics/tradesmen kept their tools in a bag at work; receiving the bag meant they were dismissed.

meanings.english: To annoy someone; to bother them continuously.
meanings.hindi: नाक में दम करना; परेशान करना।

content.usage: Stop getting in my hair while I'm trying to cook!

extras.mnemonic: Imagine having gum, lice, or tangles in your Hair (बाल). It is annoying and hard to remove. A person 'in your hair' is a constant Irritation (परेशानी) you can't shake off.
extras.origin: 19th Century. Alludes to the annoyance of having something entangled in one's hair, like lice or burrs, which is irritating and difficult to remove.

meanings.english: To accept defeat; to yield.

content.usage: After hours of arguing, he finally gave in and apologized.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Two people wrestling. One person stops fighting and Hands Over (Give) the victory to the other. To 'give in' is to stop resisting and Accept Defeat (हार मानना).
extras.origin: Phrasal verb. To 'give' implies surrendering or yielding possession (in this case, yielding one's position or resistance).

meanings.english: To die; to stop working (for machines).
meanings.hindi: मर जाना; दम तोड़ देना (मशीन का खराब होना)।

content.usage: My old car finally gave up the ghost on the highway.

extras.mnemonic: The Ghost represents the Spirit or Soul (आत्मा). When a person dies, the spirit leaves the body—they 'give up' the ghost. Can also apply to a machine 'dying'.
extras.origin: Biblical language (King James Bible, 1611). 'Ghost' meant 'spirit' or 'breath'. Dying was described as 'yielding up the ghost' (releasing the soul).

meanings.english: To collapse; to yield to pressure.

content.usage: The old bridge might give way under the weight of the truck.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a dam holding back water. If the pressure is too high, the dam Moves Aside (Gives Way) and water floods. It means to Collapse (टूट जाना) or yield.
extras.origin: Engineering/Structural terminology. When a structure can no longer support a load, it yields or 'gives way'. Also used in traffic rules (yielding right of way).

meanings.english: To go bankrupt; to fail completely.
meanings.hindi: दिवालिया हो जाना; बर्बाद हो जाना।

content.usage: During the recession, many small businesses went belly up.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A dead Fish floating in a tank. It floats upside down with its Belly Up (पेट ऊपर). If a company goes 'belly up', it is Dead/Bankrupt (बर्बाद).
extras.origin: Refers to the behavior of dead fish or other animals that die and bloat, floating with their belly upwards. It became a metaphor for business failure or bankruptcy.

meanings.english: Divide the cost; each person pays for themselves.

content.usage: We decided to go Dutch on the dinner date.

extras.mnemonic: The English and Dutch were rivals. The English mocked the Dutch as Stingy (कंजूस). A 'Dutch treat' meant you pay for yourself. 'Going Dutch' means Splitting the Bill (बिल बांटना).
extras.origin: From 17th-century Anglo-Dutch wars. The English created many derogatory idioms about the Dutch (Dutch courage, Dutch treat). 'Dutch treat' implied no treat at all, as you paid for yourself.

meanings.english: To be sold cheaply.

content.usage: Since the owner was in a hurry, the house went for a song.

extras.mnemonic: Singing a Song (गाना) is free and easy. If you buy something 'for a song', you pay almost Nothing (बहुत कम). It implies a very low price.
extras.origin: Elizabethan era. Street ballads (songs printed on paper) were sold for a penny or very little. Thus, something sold 'for a song' was sold for a trifling amount.

meanings.english: Attack all out; to attack the weakest or most vital part.
meanings.hindi: जानलेवा हमला करना; कमजोर नस दबाना।

content.usage: In the debate, he went for the jugular by attacking his opponent's personal life.

extras.mnemonic: The Jugular is the vital Vein (नस) in the neck. Predators like wolves bite the jugular to Kill (मारना) instantly. Going for the jugular means attacking the most Vulnerable (कमजोर) spot to destroy the opponent.
extras.origin: Animal predation metaphor. Predators instinctively attack the throat (jugular vein) to bring down prey quickly. It signifies an aggressive strategy aiming for total defeat.

meanings.english: To become out of control; to stop working correctly.

content.usage: The computer system went haywire after the power cut.

extras.mnemonic: Wire used to bundle Hay (सूखी घास) is stiff and springy. If you cut it, it springs out wildly in all directions. Going 'haywire' means going Crazy (पागल) or losing control.
extras.origin: American agricultural term. Baling wire (hay wire) was notoriously difficult to manage; when cut, it would spring apart in a chaotic, tangled mess.

meanings.english: A born fool.

content.usage: He behaves like God's ape, imitating others without understanding.

extras.mnemonic: A Monkey (Ape) tries to copy humans but looks silly. 'God's Ape' is a natural born Fool (मूर्ख) created by God, who can only mimic wisdom but has none.
extras.origin: Old literary term. Apes were seen as mimics of man. A 'natural' (born fool) was sometimes called 'God's ape' implies a creature that mimics human behavior without understanding.

meanings.english: A cemetery (usually beside a church).

content.usage: He was buried in God's acre behind the old chapel.

extras.mnemonic: An Acre is a unit of Land (जमीन). This specific land belongs to God because it holds the Dead (मृतक). 'God's acre' is a poetic name for a Graveyard (कब्रिस्तान).
extras.origin: Translation of the German 'Gottesacker' (God's field). It reflects the Christian belief that the bodies are 'sown' in the earth like seeds to be raised again at the Resurrection.

meanings.english: To smile a lot; to look very happy.
meanings.hindi: बाँछे खिल जाना; बहुत खुश होना।

content.usage: He was grinning from ear to ear after winning the lottery.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A smile so big and Wide (चौड़ा) that the corners of the mouth seem to touch the Ears (कान). It signifies Extreme Happiness (अत्यधिक खुशी).
extras.origin: Descriptive idiom found in 19th-century literature. It hyperbolically describes a broad smile that spans the entire width of the face.

meanings.english: To take retirement (usually from sports).

content.usage: After 20 years of football, he decided to hang up his boots.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A football player entering the locker room for the last time. He takes off his Boots (जूते) and Hangs them on a hook forever. It means Stopping (रुकना) the career.
extras.origin: Sports origin, specifically football/soccer. When a professional player retires, they literally hang up their playing boots for the last time. Similar to 'hang up the gloves' in boxing.

meanings.english: To scold severely.

content.usage: The employee was hauled over the coals for losing the important file.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Dragging (Hauling) a person over hot burning Coals (कोयला). It sounds like torture. Metaphorically, it means inflicting severe Pain/Scolding (सजा/डांट) for a mistake.
extras.origin: Historical torture/ordeal. In medieval times, heretics were sometimes dragged over live coals. Surviving meant innocence. It evolved to mean a severe reprimand.

meanings.english: To daydream; to be out of touch with reality.
meanings.hindi: ख्याली पुलाव पकाना; वास्तविकता से दूर।

content.usage: He has his head in the clouds if he thinks he can pass without studying.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A tall giant whose Head (सिर) is stuck up in the Clouds (बादल). He cannot see the ground (Reality). A person with their 'head in the clouds' is Daydreaming (सपनों में) and impractical.
extras.origin: Descriptive metaphor used since the mid-17th century. It suggests mental detachment from earthly, practical realities, floating in the realm of imagination.

meanings.english: In disorderly haste; confused and hurried.

content.usage: When the rain started, people ran helter-skelter for shelter.

extras.mnemonic: Sounds like Chaos. Also, a 'Helter-Skelter' is a spiral slide at a fairground where you slide down fast and dizzy. It implies moving in a Hurried (जल्दबाजी) and Disordered (अस्त-व्यस्त) way.
extras.origin: 16th-century rhyming compound. Likely an onomatopoeic representation of hurry and confusion. Later became the name of a fairground slide, reinforcing the idea of spiraling out of control.

meanings.english: Neglected; abandoned in a difficult situation.

content.usage: They pulled out of the deal, leaving us high and dry.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A ship stranded High (ऊपर) on the beach. It is Dry (सूखा) because the water has gone away. It cannot move. Being left 'high and dry' means being Abandoned (छोड़ दिया गया) and helpless.
extras.origin: Nautical term. Refers to a ship beached above the high-tide mark. Once the tide goes out, the ship is stranded 'high and dry' and cannot float away until the next high tide.

meanings.english: Everywhere.

content.usage: I searched high and low for my keys but couldn't find them.

extras.mnemonic: I looked in High places (shelves) and Low places (floor). I looked Everywhere (हर जगह).
extras.origin: Spatial metaphor indicating a thorough search covering all possible locations, from the ceiling to the floor.

meanings.english: To live in a luxurious way.

content.usage: After winning the lottery, they started living high on the hog.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Pig (Hog/सूअर). The best meat (loin, chops) is found High up on the back. The cheap meat (feet, belly) is low. Eating 'High on the Hog' means eating the Best (सर्वोत्तम) and living luxuriously.
extras.origin: American South culinary term. The choicest cuts of pork come from the upper parts of the pig. Wealthy people could afford these cuts, while the poor ate the lower parts (trotters, hocks).

meanings.english: To endure a difficult situation; to refuse to yield.

content.usage: The besieged city managed to hold out for three months.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Soldiers inside a fort Holding the gates shut, keeping the enemy Out (बाहर). They Refuse to Surrender (हार नहीं मानना) despite hunger or attacks.
extras.origin: Military metaphor. A garrison 'holds out' against a siege, maintaining their position and refusing to surrender to the enemy.

meanings.english: To behave in a silly and noisy way.

content.usage: Stop horsing around and finish your homework!

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Young Horses (घोड़े) playing in a field, running, kicking, and pushing each other. 'Horsing around' means behaving playfully and Roughly (मस्ती करना) like a colt.
extras.origin: Derived from 'horseplay', a term used since the 16th century to describe rough, boisterous, or practical joking behavior, likened to the physical play of horses.

meanings.english: In a nervous state; excited and confused.

content.usage: The bride was in a flutter before the wedding ceremony.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Bird. When it is scared or excited, it Flaps (Flutters/फड़फड़ाना) its wings rapidly. Being 'in a flutter' means your heart is beating fast like bird wings due to Nervousness (घबराहट).
extras.origin: Nature metaphor. Describes the physical sensation of a rapidly beating heart or trembling, likened to the fluttering of a bird's wings.

meanings.english: Something that is done very quickly; in a moment.

content.usage: I'll be back in a jiffy.

extras.mnemonic: Jiffy sounds like 'Zip'. Fast. Visual: A lightning flash. 'In a jiffy' means in a split Second (क्षण).
extras.origin: 18th Century slang. In physics/electronics, a 'jiffy' was later defined as the time it takes light to travel one centimeter, but originally it just meant an undefined, extremely short period.

meanings.english: In a partnership with (usually for dishonest purposes).

content.usage: It seems the corrupt officer was in cahoots with the smugglers.

extras.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Co-Hoots'. Imagine two Owls hooting together in the dark to plan a robbery. Or sharing a tiny Hut (Cahute). It means Conspiring (साजिश) together.
extras.origin: American slang (19th century). Possibly from the French 'cahute' (cabin/hut), implying people sharing a small space (and secrets). Or related to 'cohorte' (partnership).

meanings.english: In great difficulty; in trouble.

content.usage: He is in deep water with the tax authorities.

extras.mnemonic: If you can't swim, Shallow water is safe. Deep Water (गहरा पानी) is dangerous; you might Drown. Being 'in deep water' means being in serious Trouble (मुसीबत).
extras.origin: Biblical/Nautical metaphor. 'Deep waters' often symbolize overwhelming trouble or danger (e.g., Psalm 69: 'I am come into deep waters, where the floods overflow me').

meanings.english: Cheerless and depressed; sad.

content.usage: He has been in the blues since he failed the exam.

extras.mnemonic: Blue is the color of Sadness (उदासी). Think of 'The Blues' music, which is often sad and slow. Being 'in the blues' means feeling Low/Depressed (हताश).
extras.origin: Derived from 'Blue Devils' (18th century), referring to the depressing hallucinations of alcohol withdrawal. Later shortened to 'the blues' to describe a general state of melancholy.

meanings.english: Appearing soon; likely to happen in the near future.

content.usage: Major changes are in the offing for the company.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: You are standing on the beach. You see a Ship on the horizon (the Offing). It hasn't docked yet, but it is Visible and Coming Soon (जल्द आ रहा है).
extras.origin: Nautical term. The 'offing' is the part of the sea visible from the shore but beyond the anchoring ground. A ship 'in the offing' was approaching and expected to arrive soon.

meanings.english: Contesting the seat; having a chance to win.
meanings.hindi: दौड़ में शामिल; जीतने की संभावना रखना।

content.usage: Despite the scandal, he is still in the running for the presidency.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Race. If you are 'In the Running' (दौड़ में), you haven't quit or fallen behind. You still have a Chance (मौका) to win.
extras.origin: Sports metaphor (Horse racing/Athletics). A competitor who is still on the track and keeping pace with the leaders is 'in the running' for the prize.

meanings.english: To be in trouble.

content.usage: If I don't finish this report by 5 PM, I'll be in the soup.

extras.mnemonic: Same as 'Get into a soup' (ID 1306). Being cooked in hot Soup (सूप) is a Messy (गड़बड़) and bad situation.
extras.origin: Late 19th-century slang. Often associated with the phrase 'in the suds', referring to dishwater, implying a messy, unpleasant situation.

meanings.english: Well informed & up to date; involved in current activities.

content.usage: After a week of vacation, I read the news to get back in the swim.

extras.mnemonic: Fish swim in Schools (groups). If you are 'in the swim', you are swimming with the main group, knowing what is going on. You are not isolated on the bank.
extras.origin: Angling/Fishing metaphor. The 'swim' is the part of the river frequented by fish. To be 'in the swim' meant to be where the action/fish are.

meanings.english: In direct opposition to; in spite of danger.
meanings.hindi: कड़े विरोध के बावजूद; खतरे के सामने।

content.usage: He succeeded in the teeth of fierce opposition.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Walking straight into the Mouth/Teeth (दांत) of a tiger or a storm. You are facing the Danger (खतरा) directly and moving forward despite it.
extras.origin: Nautical or Hunting metaphor. 'In the teeth of the wind' means sailing directly against a strong gale. 'In the teeth of danger' implies facing a threat head-on.

meanings.english: Detachment and seclusion; a place of intellectual isolation from the real world.
meanings.hindi: यथार्थ से दूर (काल्पनिक दुनिया); बौद्धिक अलगाव।

content.usage: Academics in their ivory towers often don't understand the problems of common people.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A tall, white Tower made of expensive Ivory (हाथी दांत). A scholar sits at the top, far away from the dirt and noise of the Real World (असली दुनिया) below. He is Isolated (अलग-थलग).
extras.origin: Originally Biblical (Song of Solomon), but the modern meaning comes from 19th-century critic Sainte-Beuve, who described poet Alfred de Vigny as writing from a 'tour d'ivoire' (ivory tower), isolated from the messy realities of life.

meanings.english: To act hastily; to start before the appropriate time.
meanings.hindi: जल्दबाजी करना; समय से पहले शुरुआत करना।

content.usage: They jumped the gun by announcing the winner before counting all the votes.

extras.mnemonic: In a running race, the referee fires a Gun (बंदूक) to start. If you run BEFORE the bang, you 'Jump the Gun'. It means acting Too Early (जल्दबाजी).
extras.origin: Track and field athletics. Starting a race before the starter's pistol is fired causes a false start. Figuratively applied to doing anything prematurely.

meanings.english: To scold angrily; to react aggressively to what someone says.

content.usage: I only made a suggestion, there was no need for him to jump down my throat.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine you open your mouth to speak, and someone angry Tries to Jump Inside (गले में कूदना) to stop you. It describes a sudden, Violent (हिंसक) verbal attack.
extras.origin: Hyperbolic expression from the late 19th century. It describes an attack so eager and aggressive that the attacker metaphorically tries to leap inside the victim.

meanings.english: Keep oneself updated; to stay side-by-side with progress.
meanings.hindi: जानकारी रखना; जमाने के साथ चलना।

content.usage: It is important to keep abreast of the latest technological developments.

extras.mnemonic: Abreast means 'Side by Side' (Breast to Breast). Imagine running next to a car. If you 'keep abreast', you stay level with it. You don't fall behind. It means Staying Updated (अपडेट रहना).
extras.origin: Nautical/Military. Ships sailing 'abreast' or soldiers marching 'abreast' are side-by-side. To 'keep abreast of' news means to move forward at the same pace as the news, not falling behind.

meanings.english: To calm down; don't get excited or angry.

content.usage: Keep your wig on! I'll find your missing book.

extras.mnemonic: In old times, men wore Wigs (नकली बाल). If they got angry and jumped around, the wig might Fall Off. 'Keep your wig on' means: Stop moving, stay Calm (शांत).
extras.origin: 18th Century. Wigs were expensive and liable to slip if the wearer became physically agitated with rage. Telling someone to keep their wig on was advice to stay composed.

meanings.english: Remained expressionless; to display fortitude and not show emotion in trouble.
meanings.hindi: हिम्मत बनाए रखना; भावनाएँ छिपाना (रोना नहीं)।

content.usage: Despite the tragedy, he kept a stiff upper lip and comforted others.

extras.mnemonic: When you are about to Cry (रोना), your Upper Lip trembles. If you stiffen your muscle to stop the trembling, you hide your sadness. It means being Brave (बहादुर) and not showing emotion.
extras.origin: 19th Century. Associated with the British character trait of stoicism. Keeping the face composed, specifically the mouth, was a sign of courage and self-control in adversity.

meanings.english: Wasting time; waiting impatiently.
meanings.hindi: समय बर्बाद करना; इंतजार में समय काटना।

content.usage: He was left kicking his heels in the waiting room for two hours.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A bored child sitting on a high chair, swinging their legs and Kicking (लात मारना) their heels against the chair leg repeatedly. They are doing nothing but Waiting (इंतजार).
extras.origin: 18th Century. Refers to the physical restlessness of someone waiting with nothing to do, drumming or kicking their heels against the ground or furniture.

meanings.english: Friends and relatives.

content.usage: He invited all his kith and kin to the wedding.

extras.mnemonic: Old English words: 'Kith' means Known (Friends/Acquaintances/जान-पहचान), and 'Kin' means Kindred (Blood Family/परिवार). So, 'Kith and Kin' covers everyone you know and are related to.
extras.origin: Old English. 'Kith' originally meant one's native land or countrymen (knowledge/acquaintance), while 'kin' meant family. The phrase groups friends and family together.

meanings.english: Learn to do particular jobs; to understand how things work.
meanings.hindi: काम का तरीका सीखना; गुर सीखना।

content.usage: It will take a few weeks for the new trainee to learn the ropes.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A new Sailor on a sailing ship. He looks confused by the hundreds of Ropes (रस्सियां) controlling the sails. He must learn which rope does what to run the ship. It means learning the Basics (बुनियादी बातें).
extras.origin: Nautical origin. In the age of sail, a key part of a sailor's training was mastering the complex system of rigging (ropes) used to handle the sails.

meanings.english: To work or play off excess energy; to release anger or emotion.

content.usage: He went for a run to let off steam after the argument.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Steam Engine or Pressure Cooker. If the pressure gets too high, you must open a valve to Let off Steam (भाप निकालना) so it doesn't explode. Humans do this by venting Anger (गुस्सा).
extras.origin: Engineering metaphor. Steam engines have safety valves to release excess pressure (steam) to prevent explosions. Figuratively, it means releasing pent-up emotions.

meanings.english: To take it easy and relax; to behave freely.

content.usage: It's the weekend, just let your hair down and enjoy the party.

extras.mnemonic: In old days, women pinned their hair up tightly in public (Formal). When they came home to Relax (आराम), they unpinned it and 'let their hair down'. It means becoming Informal (अनौपचारिक) and free.
extras.origin: 17th Century social custom. Women wore their hair pinned up in elaborate styles in public. Letting it hang loose was reserved for the privacy of the bedroom or dressing room, signifying relaxation.

content.phrase: Like a shag on a rock

meanings.english: Completely alone; exposed.

content.usage: I was left standing there like a shag on a rock while everyone else left.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Shag' is a seabird (Cormorant). Visual: A single black bird sitting all Alone (अकेला) on a rock in the ocean, drying its wings. It looks isolated and Exposed (उजागर).
extras.origin: Australian slang. It refers to the cormorant (often called a shag) which is frequently seen sitting solitary on coastal rocks to dry its plumage.

meanings.english: Completely; entirely.

content.usage: He sold the business lock, stock, and barrel.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: An old Musket Gun. It has three main parts: the Lock (firing mechanism), the Stock (wooden handle), and the Barrel (metal tube). Selling 'Lock, Stock, and Barrel' means selling the Entire (पूरा) gun—everything.
extras.origin: Firearms terminology. An early musket consisted of these three main components. The phrase signifies the entirety of a thing, leaving nothing behind.

meanings.english: Be careful; watch out.

content.usage: Look out! There is a car coming.

extras.mnemonic: If you are inside a safe house, you are fine. But if danger is coming, someone yells 'Look Out' (बाहर देखो) to see the approaching threat. It means Be Careful (सावधान).
extras.origin: Phrasal verb dating back to the 15th century. It implies directing one's gaze outward to check for danger.

meanings.english: Ran away with; departed hastily.

content.usage: The thieves made off with the jewelry before the police arrived.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a thief who 'Makes' a plan and then goes 'Off' (Away/दूर). 'Made off' implies leaving a scene quickly, usually with stolen goods (Escape/भागना).
extras.origin: Phrasal verb usage. To 'make' in this sense means to proceed or go (as in 'make for the exit'). 'Off' implies away.

meanings.english: Entirely false & fabricated.

content.usage: His excuse for being late was made out of whole cloth.

extras.mnemonic: Tailors cut pieces of cloth to make a suit. A story 'made out of whole cloth' isn't patched together from truth; it is Fabricated (मनगढ़ंत) entirely from scratch, like a new piece of fabric. It is a Lie (झूठ).
extras.origin: American origin (early 19th century). Originally, 'whole cloth' meant cloth of the full width and unpatched quality (positive). Ironically, it shifted to mean a lie manufactured entirely from scratch, with no element of truth.

meanings.english: To pretend; to imagine.

content.usage: Children often make believe that they are superheroes.
