extras.mnemonic: You 'Make' yourself 'Believe' (यकीन करना) something that isn't real. Like children playing house or superheroes. It is purely Pretend (नाटक).
extras.origin: First recorded in the 14th century. It describes the act of feigning or imagining a situation to be true, commonly associated with children's play.

meanings.english: Run away with something stolen.

content.usage: The cat made off with the fish left on the table.

extras.mnemonic: See 'Made off'. You run away (Make off) 'With' something in your hands. It implies Theft (चोरी) and escape.
extras.origin: See 'Made off'. The addition of 'with' specifies that something was taken during the escape.

meanings.english: Afraid of speaking frankly; speaking vaguely or indirectly.
meanings.hindi: स्पष्ट बोलने से डरने वाला; गोलमोल बातें करने वाला।

content.usage: Politicians often give mealy-mouthed answers to difficult questions.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine trying to speak with your Mouth full of dry Meal (Flour/आटा). Your words would be soft, muffled, and Unclear (अस्पष्ट). A 'mealy-mouthed' person refuses to speak plainly or boldly.
extras.origin: 16th Century. 'Meal' is a soft powder. The phrase suggests speech that is soft, powdery, and lacking the 'bite' or crispness of truth. It implies unwillingness to state facts plainly.

content.phrase: Nail your colours to the mast

meanings.english: To tell what you are thinking; to declare one's position defiantly.
meanings.hindi: अपना मत स्पष्ट करना; पीछे हटने से इनकार करना।

content.usage: During the debate, he nailed his colours to the mast and supported the controversial bill.

extras.mnemonic: In a sea battle, if you lower your Flag (Colours), you Surrender. If you Nail (कील ठोकना) the flag to the pole (Mast), it cannot be lowered. It means you will Never Surrender (कभी हार नहीं मानना) and stand by your opinion.
extras.origin: Naval warfare history. A ship's flag (colours) was struck (lowered) to signal surrender. Nailing it to the mast made surrender physically impossible, showing total defiance and commitment.

meanings.english: No chance of success; a refusal.
meanings.hindi: कोई संभावना नहीं; सफलता न मिलना।

content.usage: I asked for a refund, but it was no dice.

extras.mnemonic: In a board game, if the Dice (पासा) fall off the table or don't land flat, the throw doesn't count. 'No Dice' means No Result (कोई परिणाम नहीं) or 'It's not happening'.
extras.origin: American gambling slang (early 20th century). If a roll of the dice was invalid or didn't count, the dealer would call 'no dice'. It came to mean a refusal or failure.

meanings.english: No one is self-sufficient; everyone relies on others.
meanings.hindi: कोई भी व्यक्ति अकेला नहीं रह सकता (समाज की जरूरत होती है)।

content.usage: You need to learn to work in a team because no man is an island.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: An Island (टापू) stands alone in the sea, disconnected. But humans are part of a Continent (महाद्वीप), connected to each other. No human can survive totally Alone (अकेला).
extras.origin: From John Donne's 'Devotions upon Emergent Occasions' (1624): 'No man is an island, entire of itself; every man is a piece of the continent'. It emphasizes human interconnectedness.

meanings.english: Tell your opinion clearly; to speak bluntly without softening the blow.

content.usage: The doctor did not mince matters and told him his condition was serious.

extras.mnemonic: To Mince meat is to chop it into tiny, soft pieces so it's easy to swallow. If you 'do not mince' your words, you serve the truth Whole (साबुत) and Hard. You speak Bluntly (सीधा/सपाट).
extras.origin: Culinary metaphor. Mincing meat makes it more palatable and easier to chew. Not mincing words means delivering the message in its raw, unsoftened form.

meanings.english: Of the best quality.

content.usage: He is a diamond of the first water; honest and brilliant.

extras.mnemonic: Diamonds are graded by how clear they are, like pure Water (पानी). A diamond of the 'First Water' is the clearest and most flawless. It means Highest Quality (सर्वोत्तम गुणवत्ता).
extras.origin: Gemology term from the 17th century. Diamonds were graded for transparency and luster. The highest grade was 'first water', followed by second and third. Applied metaphorically to excellence.

meanings.english: Without preparation; impromptu.

content.usage: He gave a brilliant speech right off the cuff.

extras.mnemonic: In the past, if a speaker forgot his lines, he might write quick notes on his stiff shirt Cuff (कलाई/आस्तीन). Speaking 'off the cuff' means relying on quick notes or improv, Not Prepared (बिना तैयारी) beforehand.
extras.origin: Refers to the habit of after-dinner speakers writing notes on their starched shirt cuffs at the last minute. It signifies unrehearsed or spontaneous speech.

meanings.english: Out of trouble; no longer held responsible.
meanings.hindi: मुसीबत से मुक्त; जिम्मेदारी से बरी।

content.usage: Since they found the real thief, you are off the hook.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A Fish caught on a Hook (कांटा). It is in big trouble (about to be eaten). If it wiggles Free (आजाद), it is 'Off the Hook'. It has escaped the danger/responsibility.
extras.origin: Fishing metaphor. A fish that manages to free itself from the angler's hook is safe. Metaphorically, a person released from a difficult obligation or blame is 'off the hook'.

meanings.english: On foot; walking.

content.usage: We missed the bus, so we had to go on Shank's mare.

extras.mnemonic: 'Shank' refers to your Shin bone (leg). 'Mare' is a female Horse (घोड़ी). If you have no horse, you must ride your own legs. 'Shank's Mare' is a humorous way to say Walking (पैदल).
extras.origin: Scottish origin (18th century 'Shanks's naig'). It is a punning reference to using one's own legs (shanks) as a mount (mare) when no horse is available.

meanings.english: On credit.

content.usage: He drank all night on the cuff and promised to pay later.

extras.mnemonic: Bartenders in old days wore stiff white shirts. If a customer couldn't pay, the bartender penciled the Debt (उधार) on his starched Cuff (आस्तीन) to remember it. 'On the cuff' means On Credit.
extras.origin: American slang (early 20th century). Bartenders or waiters would temporarily record a patron's tab on their stiff paper or starched cuffs.

meanings.english: Honest; truthful.

content.usage: Are you sure this deal is on the level?

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A Carpenter using a Spirit Level tool to check if a surface is Flat (समतल). If it is level, it is straight and true. 'On the level' means Honest (ईमानदार) and not 'crooked'.
extras.origin: Freemasonry origin. The 'level' is a Masonic symbol for equality and integrity. In carpentry, a level surface is true and reliable, unlike a crooked or sloped one.

meanings.english: Totally; completely (often used for negative things).

content.usage: That is an out and out lie!

extras.mnemonic: Imagine something inside turning completely inside-out. 'Out and Out' means Thoroughly (पूरी तरह से). An 'out and out lie' is a lie from the inside out—completely fake.
extras.origin: 14th Century. Originally meant 'outwardly' or 'thoroughly'. It emphasizes completeness, often used to describe something absolute (like a lie or a scoundrel).

meanings.english: Poor; shabby.

content.usage: After losing his job, he was looking quite out at the elbows.

extras.mnemonic: When you are too Poor (गरीब) to buy new clothes, your old coat wears thin. The first place holes appear is the Elbows (कोहनी). Being 'out at the elbows' means being Shabby (फटेहाल).
extras.origin: Refers to worn-out clothing. A coat that is worn through at the elbows is a classic sign of poverty or shabbiness.

meanings.english: Forbidden; outside the allowed area.

content.usage: The staff room is out of bounds for students.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Football or Cricket field. There is a boundary line. If the ball or player goes 'Out of Bounds' (सीमा से बाहर), play stops. It is a Forbidden (वर्जित) area.
extras.origin: Sports/Geographical terminology. Originally referred to the physical limits of a parish or property. In sports, it denotes the area outside the playing field where action cannot occur.

meanings.english: Out of danger or difficulty.

content.usage: The patient is stable, but he is not out of the woods yet.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Being lost in a dark, scary Forest (Woods/जंगल) full of wolves. When you finally walk out into the open field, you are Safe (सुरक्षित). 'Out of the woods' means the Danger is over.
extras.origin: Survival metaphor used since Roman times. Forests were places of danger (bandits, beasts, getting lost). Emerging from the forest signified reaching safety.

meanings.english: Completely; deeply (usually in love or debt).

content.usage: He fell over head and ears in love with her.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Jumping into a deep pool. The water covers your toes, then your waist, and finally goes 'Over your Head and Ears' (सिर और कान के ऊपर). You are Completely Submerged (पूरी तरह डूबा हुआ) in love or debt.
extras.origin: Variation of 'over head and heels'. It describes total immersion in water, metaphorically applied to being overwhelmed by debt or emotion.

meanings.english: To dispose off with the intent to deceive; to pass off something fake as real.

content.usage: The shopkeeper tried to palm off a fake watch on me.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Magician. He hides a coin in his Palm (हथेली) to trick you. To 'Palm Off' something means using a trick to hand over something Fake (नकली) or unwanted to someone else.
extras.origin: From sleight of hand magic or dice cheating. Magicians conceal items in their palm. To 'palm off' meant to trick someone into accepting a card or die they didn't choose.

meanings.english: A prolific source of trouble; a process that generates many complicated problems.

content.usage: Opening that investigation opened a Pandora's box of corruption charges.

extras.mnemonic: Myth: Pandora was given a Box (पिटारा) and told never to open it. Curiosity won, she opened it, and all the Evils/Troubles (मुसीबतें) of the world flew out. Opening it means inviting Chaos.
extras.origin: Greek Mythology. Pandora was the first woman. Zeus gave her a jar (later mistranslated as a box) containing all human evils. When she opened it, misery was released into the world, leaving only Hope inside.

meanings.english: An unpleasant remark at the end of a conversation as one leaves.

content.usage: As he slammed the door, he fired a Parthian shot about her cooking.

extras.mnemonic: The Parthians were ancient warriors. They would pretend to Retreat (पीछे हटना) on horses, then turn around and Shoot arrows backward at the enemy. A 'Parthian shot' is an insult thrown while Leaving (जाते समय).
extras.origin: Historical military tactic of the Parthians (ancient Iran). Their mounted archers were famous for firing arrows backward while retreating. Originally 'Parthian shot', often corrupted to 'parting shot'.

meanings.english: Very similar; indistinguishable.

content.usage: Those twins are like two peas in a pod.

extras.mnemonic: Open a Pod of Peas (मटर की फली). All the little green peas inside are round, green, and Identical (एक जैसे). If two people are 'peas in a pod', they are Exactly Alike.
extras.origin: Nature metaphor. Peas grown within the same pod are virtually indistinguishable from one another in size and color.

meanings.english: Analyse critically; to find many faults with.
meanings.hindi: बाल की खाल निकालना; कड़ी आलोचना करना।

content.usage: The critics picked his new novel to pieces.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a child with a toy. He Takes it Apart (Pieces/टुकड़े) to see how it works or to destroy it. To 'pick to pieces' an idea is to Analyze (विश्लेषण) it destructively until nothing good is left.
extras.origin: Destructive analysis metaphor. Similar to 'tearing something apart'. It implies dismantling an argument or work bit by bit to expose every fault.

meanings.english: Something not possible; an illusory promise of future happiness.

content.usage: His plans for a Mars colony are just pie in the sky right now.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a delicious Pie (पाई/भोजन) floating high in the Sky. You want it, but you can never Reach it. It is a lovely dream but Impossible (असंभव) to get.
extras.origin: From the 1911 song 'The Preacher and the Slave' by Joe Hill. It mocked preachers who told hungry workers not to complain because they would get 'pie in the sky when you die' (rewards in heaven).

meanings.english: Hunt for a job on the street; to walk the streets looking for work.
meanings.hindi: नौकरी की तलाश में दर-दर भटकना।

content.usage: He has been pounding the pavement for months looking for a job.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Walking heavily (Pounding) on the hard Concrete (Pavement/सड़क). It signifies the hard, physical work of walking door-to-door searching for a Job (नौकरी).
extras.origin: Refers to the repetitive, heavy steps taken while walking long distances on city streets, typically when searching for employment or selling goods.

meanings.english: To trick someone; to deceive.

content.usage: Don't try to pull a fast one on me; I know what you're up to.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Magician or Cheater. He moves his hands So Fast (तेज) that your eyes can't follow the trick. 'Pulling a fast one' means doing something sneaky quickly to Deceive (धोखा देना) someone.
extras.origin: Early 20th-century slang, likely from sports (baseball) or gambling/con games, referring to a swift, deceptive move that catches the opponent off guard.

meanings.english: To use personal influence to get something done.
meanings.hindi: जुगाड़ लगाना; प्रभाव का इस्तेमाल करना।

content.usage: He got the job because his father pulled some strings.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Puppet Show. The puppet moves, but the audience can't see the strings. The Puppeteer (controller) 'Pulls Strings' (डोर खींचना) to make things happen. It means using hidden Influence (प्रभाव).
extras.origin: Puppetry metaphor. A marionette is controlled by strings from above. Figuratively, it means manipulating events or people from behind the scenes.

meanings.english: To calm oneself down; to regain control of emotions.

content.usage: Stop crying and pull yourself together!

extras.mnemonic: When you are hysterical, you feel like you are 'falling apart' or breaking into pieces. To fix this, you must Gather (Pull) your pieces Together (इकट्ठा करना). It means regaining Composure (संयम).
extras.origin: 19th Century. Based on the metaphor of a person 'falling apart' under stress. 'Pulling oneself together' is the act of reassembling one's mental state.

meanings.english: Tolerate; to endure.

content.usage: I cannot put up with this noise any longer.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a guest arrives. You have to 'Put them Up' (House them/ठहराना). They might be annoying, but you must Endure (बर्दाश्त करना) them. To 'put up with' means to tolerate something unpleasant.
extras.origin: Phrasal verb. Originally 'to put up' meant to pocket or sheath (sword), later 'to house/lodge'. 'Putting up with' implies bearing the burden of something/someone.

meanings.english: Meet accidentally.

content.usage: I ran into my old teacher at the supermarket.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: You are running around a corner and accidentally Bump (Run/टकराना) into an old friend. You didn't plan it. It means Meeting Unexpectedly (अचानक मिलना).
extras.origin: Phrasal verb. Originally meant a literal physical collision ('run into a wall'). Figuratively extended to accidental meetings with people.

meanings.english: Act without restraint; to grow or behave uncontrollably.

content.usage: The weeds have run riot in the garden.

extras.mnemonic: A Riot (दंगा) is a chaotic, uncontrolled crowd. If weeds or children 'Run Riot', they are growing or behaving wildly without any Control (नियंत्रण) or discipline.
extras.origin: Hunting term. When hounds lost the scent of the intended prey and started chasing any random animal they saw, they were said to be 'running riot' (acting without discipline).

meanings.english: Face negative consequences of one's actions.

content.usage: The government ignored the protests, and now they are reaping the whirlwind.

extras.mnemonic: Farming logic: If you Sow (plant) Wind, you will Harvest (Reap/काटना) a Storm (Whirlwind/तूफान). Small bad actions grow into huge Disasters (बड़ी मुसीबत).
extras.origin: Biblical (Hosea 8:7): 'For they have sown the wind, and they shall reap the whirlwind.' Warning that foolish actions lead to vastly more destructive consequences.

meanings.english: To revive forgotten quarrels; to bring up old bad memories.

content.usage: Let's not rip up old sores; we need to move forward.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a healed wound (Sore/घाव). If you Rip (tear/उखाड़ना) the scab off, it bleeds and hurts again. Bringing up old fights hurts people again just like reopening a wound.
extras.origin: Medical metaphor. Reopening a healing wound causes fresh pain. Figuratively, it means renewing old grievances or quarrels that had settled.

meanings.english: Completely; destroying something totally.

content.usage: The new manager decided to reorganize the company root and branch.

extras.mnemonic: To destroy a Tree completely, you don't just cut the leaves. You pull out the Roots (जड़) and cut every Branch (शाखा). It means Total (पूर्ण) removal or change.
extras.origin: Biblical origin (Malachi 4:1): '...it shall leave them neither root nor branch.' It signifies total destruction or complete reform.

meanings.english: A positive outlook on life; seeing things as better than they are.

content.usage: He looks at the world through rose-coloured glasses and refuses to see the danger.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Wearing sunglasses with Pink/Rose lenses. Suddenly, the gray, ugly world looks warm and rosy. It means seeing only the Good (अच्छा) and ignoring the bad reality.
extras.origin: Mid-19th Century. Looking through rose-tinted glass makes objects appear warm and pleasant. Figuratively, it refers to an unduly optimistic or naive perspective.

meanings.english: Adolescence; time of youth and inexperience.
meanings.hindi: जवानी के दिन; अनुभवहीनता का दौर।

content.usage: He often talks about his salad days when he was a struggling artist.

extras.mnemonic: Salad is made of Green, fresh vegetables. In idioms, 'Green' means Young and Inexperienced (हरा/कच्चा). 'Salad Days' are your youthful days when you were fresh but naive.
extras.origin: From Shakespeare's 'Antony and Cleopatra' (1606). Cleopatra refers to her youth as 'My salad days, When I was green in judgment'.

meanings.english: Deal firmly with a difficulty.

content.usage: We need to seize the nettle and solve this debt problem now.

extras.mnemonic: A Nettle (बिछुआ) is a plant with stinging hairs. If you touch it gently, it stings. But if you Grab (Seize/पकड़ना) it firmly, the thorns are crushed and don't hurt. It means tackling a problem Boldly (साहस से).
extras.origin: From an old proverb: 'Grasp the nettle and it won't sting you'. It suggests that hesitation causes pain, while bold, decisive action conquers the difficulty.

meanings.english: To get rid of something bad (like a cold or a bad habit).

content.usage: I can't seem to shake off this cold.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A wet Dog coming out of water. It Shakes (झटकना) its body violently to throw the water drops Off. To 'shake off' a problem is to Get Rid (छुटकारा पाना) of it vigorously.
extras.origin: Physical metaphor derived from animals shaking themselves to remove water, dust, or parasites.

meanings.english: Very easy task.
meanings.hindi: बाएं हाथ का खेल; बहुत आसान काम।

content.usage: The quiz was so easy, it was like shooting fish in a barrel.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine trying to shoot a fish in a huge river (Hard). Now imagine Fish trapped inside a small Barrel (ड्रम). They have nowhere to go. You can't miss. It is Super Easy (बहुत आसान).
extras.origin: Early 20th Century. If fish are packed into a barrel for storage or transport, shooting them requires no skill whatsoever. It signifies an effortless task.

meanings.english: A simple solution to a complicated problem.

content.usage: There is no silver bullet for climate change; it requires many different solutions.

extras.mnemonic: In monster stories, a Werewolf cannot be killed by normal guns. Only a Bullet made of Silver (चांदी की गोली) can kill it instantly. It represents a Magical Solution (जादुई उपाय) to a tough problem.
extras.origin: Folklore. Legend held that silver bullets were the only weapon effective against werewolves, witches, or other supernatural monsters. In modern usage, it means a quick, miraculous fix.

meanings.english: To wait before making a decision.
meanings.hindi: निर्णय लेने से पहले सोच-विचार करना (अगले दिन तक टालना)।

content.usage: Don't decide now; sleep on it and tell me your answer tomorrow.

extras.mnemonic: Don't decide when you are tired or angry. Go to Sleep (सोना). Let your brain process the problem overnight. In the morning, with a fresh mind, the answer will be clear. Delay the Decision (फैसला टालना).
extras.origin: Based on the common experience that a night's rest clears the mind and allows the subconscious to process problems, leading to better decisions the next morning.

meanings.english: Indifferent; aloof and unfriendly.

content.usage: She seems stand-offish, but she is actually just shy.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a ship that 'Stands Off' the shore. It stays in deep water and Refuses to Dock (किनारे नहीं आता). A 'stand-offish' person keeps their Distance (दूरी) and refuses to be friendly.
extras.origin: Nautical term. A ship 'stands off' when it maintains a distance from the shore or another vessel. Applied to people, it means keeping a social distance or being aloof.

meanings.english: Complete halt; a state of no movement.

content.usage: Traffic came to a standstill due to the accident.

extras.mnemonic: A compound word: Stand + Still. If you Stand (खड़े होना) perfectly Still (शांत), there is Zero Movement. Traffic at a standstill is not moving at all.
extras.origin: Compound word usage implying a state of total cessation of movement or activity.

meanings.english: An indication of what might happen; a sign of future trends.

content.usage: The small protest was just a straw in the wind representing larger unrest.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: You want to know which way the Wind (हवा) is blowing, but you can't see wind. You throw a light piece of Straw (तिनका) in the air. Its movement Indicates (संकेत देना) the wind's direction.
extras.origin: From the proverb: 'A straw will show which way the wind blows'. Before modern instruments, tossing light objects like straw was a practical way to gauge wind direction. Figuratively, a small sign of a larger trend.

meanings.english: Pride; conceited.
meanings.hindi: घमंडी; जिसका दिमाग सातवें आसमान पर हो।

content.usage: Success has made him swollen headed; he won't talk to his old friends.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a person whose Ego (अहंकार) is so big that their Head physically Swells (सूजना) up like a balloon. A 'swollen-headed' person is full of Pride (घमंड).
extras.origin: Colloquial metaphor. Similar to 'big-headed'. It implies the person's sense of self-importance has expanded beyond normal size.

meanings.english: To object strongly; to be offended by.

content.usage: I take exception to your comment that I am lazy.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine someone listing facts. You stop them and say, 'I accept everything EXCEPT (सिवाय) this one point'. To 'take exception' is to single something out to Object (विरोध करना) or be offended by it.
extras.origin: Legal terminology. In court, a lawyer would 'take exception' (make a formal objection) to a judge's ruling or statement.

meanings.english: To take courage; to feel encouraged.

content.usage: Take heart, things will get better soon.

extras.mnemonic: The Heart (दिल) is the symbol of Courage and spirit. If you have lost hope, you need to 'Take Heart' (courage) back into your body. Be Brave (बहादुर) and hopeful.
extras.origin: Old English. The heart was considered the seat of courage and intellect. 'Taking heart' meant gathering one's spirit and confidence.

meanings.english: Prepare to go to war.

content.usage: The tribes decided to take up the hatchet to defend their land.

extras.mnemonic: Native Americans Buried the Hatchet (axe) to make Peace. The opposite is digging it up or 'Taking up the Hatchet' (कुल्हाड़ी उठाना). It means preparing for War (युद्ध).
extras.origin: Native American custom. A hatchet (tomahawk) was buried to symbolize peace. Taking it up or digging it up signified declaring war.

meanings.english: Similar to (usually a parent or relative).
meanings.hindi: शक्ल या स्वभाव में मिलना (अपने बड़ों जैसा होना)।

content.usage: She takes after her mother in her love for music.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine you are following 'After' your father on a path. You walk like him and look like him. 'Taking after' means resembling a parent in Looks or Behavior (स्वभाव).
extras.origin: Phrasal verb. To follow the example or nature of an ancestor.

meanings.english: Majority of votes are in favour.
meanings.hindi: प्रस्ताव के पक्ष में बहुमत होना।

content.usage: The Speaker announced, 'The ayes have it', and the bill was passed.

extras.mnemonic: 'Aye' is an old word for Yes (हां). In Parliament, people shout 'Aye' or 'No'. If the 'Ayes' (Yes voters) are louder/more, they Have (win) the vote. The motion passes.
extras.origin: Parliamentary procedure (British House of Commons). The Speaker determines the result of a voice vote by deciding whether the 'Ayes' (Yes) or 'Noes' are in the majority.

meanings.english: Extraordinary; outstandingly good.

content.usage: He thinks his new motorcycle is the bee's knees.

extras.mnemonic: Bees carry sweet pollen in little sacs on their Knees (घुटने). Since pollen is good/sweet, the 'bee's knees' became slang for something Excellent (बेहतरीन). (Also just a rhyming joke).
extras.origin: 1920s slang. One of many nonsense animal phrases used to denote excellence (like 'the cat's pajamas'). It may loosely relate to the goodness of pollen carried on a bee's legs, but was primarily a linguistic fad.

meanings.english: The decision has been taken and cannot be changed.
meanings.hindi: फैसला हो चुका है (अब पीछे नहीं हटो जा सकता)।

content.usage: We have signed the contract; the die is cast.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Die' (singular of Dice) is thrown (Cast/फेंकना) in a game. Once it leaves your hand, you cannot stop it or change the number. The action is Final (अंतिम).
extras.origin: Said by Julius Caesar ('Alea iacta est') upon crossing the Rubicon river with his army in 49 BC. It meant he had committed to civil war and there was no turning back.

meanings.english: Big international bankers (specifically Swiss bankers).
meanings.hindi: स्विस बैंकर (जो गुप्त रूप से धन रखते हैं)।

content.usage: The Gnomes of Zurich were blamed for the currency crisis.

extras.mnemonic: Gnomes are mythical dwarfs who guard Treasure (खजाना) underground. Swiss Bankers keep huge wealth in underground vaults in Zurich. They are secretive money guardians.
extras.origin: Coined by British politician Harold Wilson in 1956. He disparagingly referred to Swiss bankers as gnomes because of their secrecy and their underground vaults filled with gold.

meanings.english: The most important aspect or practical details of a subject.

content.usage: Let's stop dreaming and get down to the nitty-gritty of the business plan.

extras.mnemonic: Gritty implies Sand/Dirt (रेत). 'Nits' are lice eggs. Getting down to the 'nitty gritty' means dealing with the unpleasant, rough, Basic Details (बुनियादी बातें) at the bottom of the problem.
extras.origin: Uncertain origin, likely mid-20th century African-American slang. It might refer to the debris (nits and grit) left at the bottom of a barrel or scalp, signifying the essential, unvarnished reality.

meanings.english: The unpleasant, immoral, or rough aspect of life.
meanings.hindi: जीवन का घिनौना या अनैतिक पहलू।

content.usage: As a police officer, he sees the seamy side of the city every night.

extras.mnemonic: Look at a shirt inside out. You see the Seams (सिलाई) where pieces join. It looks messy and ugly compared to the smooth outside. The 'seamy side' is the Hidden, Ugly Reality (घिनौना सच).
extras.origin: Shakespearean origin ('Othello'). A garment worn inside out shows the seams, which are rough and unfinished. It metaphorically refers to the sordid, less attractive reality hidden behind a polite facade.

meanings.english: Start of a harmful development; a small change that leads to bigger problems.

content.usage: Ignoring minor rule violations is the thin end of the wedge for total lawlessness.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A Wedge is a tool shaped like a triangle. If the Thin End (पतला सिरा) enters a crack in a log, the Thick End will follow and Split the wood. A small start leads to a Big Consequence (बड़ा अंजाम).
extras.origin: Mechanical metaphor. A wedge allows a small force to split a large object. Figuratively, it refers to a minor action or precedent that inevitably leads to a major, often undesirable, result.

meanings.english: Things starting to go wrong; a situation failing completely.
meanings.hindi: गाड़ी पटरी से उतर जाना; सब कुछ बिगड़ जाना।

content.usage: The project was going well until the funding stopped, and then the wheels came off.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A fast-moving Car. Suddenly, the Wheels (पहिये) fall off. The car crashes and stops. When the 'wheels come off' a plan, it Fails Completely (असफल) and stops moving forward.
extras.origin: Vehicle metaphor. A vehicle cannot function without wheels. Used to describe a process, organization, or person that suddenly ceases to function effectively or falls into chaos.

meanings.english: To prevent a plan from succeeding; to sabotage.
meanings.hindi: रंग में भंग डालना; काम बिगाड़ना।

content.usage: The sudden rain threw a spanner in our picnic plans.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Gears of a machine spinning smoothly. Someone throws a heavy metal Tool (Spanner/Wrench) into the gears. CRASH! The machine stops. It means Sabotaging (बिगाड़ना) a plan.
extras.origin: Industrial metaphor. Throwing a tool (spanner in UK, monkey wrench in US) into moving machinery would cause it to jam or break. Figuratively means causing a disruption.

meanings.english: To express hatred or disrespect; to show contempt.

content.usage: He thumbed his nose at the rules and did whatever he wanted.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Putting your Thumb on your Nose and wiggling your fingers at someone. It is a childish, rude Gesture (इशारा) of Mockery (मजाक). It means showing Disrespect.
extras.origin: Refers to the physical gesture known as 'cocking a snook'. It is a traditional gesture of derision or contempt.

meanings.english: To surrender; to give up.

content.usage: After struggling for years, he finally threw up the sponge and closed the business.

extras.mnemonic: Boxing: Between rounds, coaches use a wet Sponge to wipe the fighter's face. If they 'Throw the Sponge' into the ring during the fight, it signals they Quit (हार मानना). Similar to throwing the towel.
extras.origin: Boxing terminology (19th century). Seconds would throw the sponge used to clean the fighter into the center of the ring to signal surrender, ending the fight.

meanings.english: For a long time; indefinitely.

content.usage: You can argue til the cows come home, but I won't change my mind.

extras.mnemonic: Cows graze in fields all day. They walk back to the barn very Slowly (धीरे) at night. Waiting 'til the cows come home' means waiting a very Long Time (लंबा समय).
extras.origin: Agricultural idiom dating back to the 16th century. Cows are placid animals that return for milking late in the day or at their own slow pace, signifying a long duration.

meanings.english: To fish; to try to get something indirectly.
meanings.hindi: मछली पकड़ना; घुमा-फिरा कर कुछ प्राप्त करने की कोशिश करना।

content.usage: He was angling for an invitation to the party.

extras.mnemonic: An 'Angle' is a Fishing Hook. To 'angle' means to fish. Metaphorically, if you 'angle' for a compliment or invitation, you are casting a line hoping to Catch (पकड़ना) it slyly.
extras.origin: From Old English 'angel' meaning a fish-hook. Fishing with a hook is called 'angling'. Figuratively, it means using artifice or indirect means to obtain something.

meanings.english: In a confusing situation; unsure what to do.

content.usage: I was in a quandary about which job offer to accept.

extras.mnemonic: Sounds like 'Wandering'. Your mind is Wandering (भटकना) between choices. 'Quandary' implies you are Stuck (फँसा हुआ) and don't know which way to go.
extras.origin: Uncertain, possibly from Latin 'quando' (when?). It implies a state of perplexity or uncertainty about what to do next.

meanings.english: To be dead and buried.

content.usage: If I try to ride that motorcycle, I'll be pushing up daisies.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A grave. The dead body is Buried (दफन) underneath. Flowers (Daisies) grow on top. From underground, the dead person acts like fertilizer 'Pushing Up' the flowers. It means being Dead (मृत).
extras.origin: World War I slang. Referring to the wildflowers (daisies) that would grow over the graves of the fallen soldiers. It's a euphemism for death.

meanings.english: To be surprised or shocked.

content.usage: I was taken aback by his sudden rudeness.

extras.mnemonic: Nautical: If the wind suddenly hits the sails from the front, the ship is pushed Backwards (Taken Aback). This is a sudden Shock (झटका) for the sailors. It means being unexpectedly Surprised (हैरान).
extras.origin: Nautical term. If the wind caught the sails on the wrong side (aback), the ship would suddenly lose momentum or be driven backward. Metaphorically, it means being arrested in progress by surprise.

meanings.english: To feel sick.

content.usage: I'm feeling a bit under the weather, so I'll stay home today.

extras.mnemonic: On a ship, if there was bad Weather (storm/waves), sailors felt Seasick. They went 'Under' the deck to rest away from the weather. It means feeling Ill (बीमार).
extras.origin: Nautical origin. Sailors who felt seasick or ill during a storm would go below deck (under the weather rail) to recover and escape the harsh elements.

meanings.english: To run away in fear; to withdraw.

content.usage: When he saw the angry dog, he beat a hasty retreat.

extras.mnemonic: In old armies, a Drummer would Beat (बजाना) the drums to signal 'Retreat' (withdraw). Soldiers hearing the beat would Run Away (भागना). Today, it just means leaving quickly.
extras.origin: Military terminology. Specific drum beats signaled commands to the troops. 'Beating a retreat' was the drum signal ordering soldiers to withdraw from the battlefield.

meanings.english: To be acquitted of a crime; to escape punishment.

content.usage: He hired a good lawyer and managed to beat the rap.

extras.mnemonic: 'Rap' is slang for a Criminal Charge (आरोप). 'Beating' it means winning against it. If you 'beat the rap', you avoid Prison (जेल) or punishment.
extras.origin: American criminal slang (early 20th century). A 'rap' meant a criminal accusation or prison sentence. Beating the rap meant evading the punishment.

meanings.english: To wish good luck (usually before a performance).

content.usage: Break a leg in your play tonight!

extras.mnemonic: Actors are superstitious. They think saying 'Good Luck' brings Bad Luck. So they say the opposite: 'Break a Leg' (टांग तोड़ो) to wish for Good Luck (शुभकामनाएं).
extras.origin: Theatrical superstition. Folklore suggests that wishing for bad luck would trick the 'evil spirits' or 'sprites' into granting the opposite (good luck).

meanings.english: To succeed; to win a victory.

content.usage: Despite strong opposition, his argument carried the day.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Battle that lasts all day. The winner 'Carries' the trophy at the end of the Day. 'Carrying the day' means Winning (जीतना) the contest.
extras.origin: Military origin. 'Day' refers to the day of battle. To carry the day meant to win the battle fought on that specific day.

meanings.english: To remove obstructions; to prepare for action.

content.usage: We need to clear the decks before starting the new project.

extras.mnemonic: Before a sea battle, sailors Clear the Decks (डेक साफ करना) of all loose ropes and items so they can fight without tripping. It means getting Ready (तैयार) for action by removing clutter.
extras.origin: Naval terminology. 'Clear for action' involved removing everything from the decks that might hinder the gun crews or cause injury during battle.

meanings.english: To gain experience at a beginning stage.

content.usage: I cut my teeth on local reporting before moving to a national newspaper.

extras.mnemonic: Babies 'Cut' their first Teeth (दांत निकलना) when they start chewing solid food. It represents the Early Stages (शुरुआत) of learning and gaining experience in life.
extras.origin: Refers to the emergence of baby teeth. Just as a baby uses its first teeth to learn how to chew, a beginner uses early tasks to gain initial experience.

meanings.english: To stop talking and start working; stop talking nonsense.
meanings.hindi: बकवास बंद करना और काम पर लगना।

content.usage: Let's cut the cackle and get down to business.

extras.mnemonic: Hens make a noisy 'Cackle' sound. It is just Noise (शोर). 'Cutting the cackle' means stopping the useless Chatter (बकवास) and getting to the important work.
extras.origin: Derived from the country saying 'Cut the cackle and come to the 'osses (horses)', meaning stop the idle chatter and get to the main business (showing the horses).

meanings.english: Come to know (usually a secret).

content.usage: I hope the press doesn't get wind of the scandal.

extras.mnemonic: Animals (like deer) smell a hunter if the Wind blows the Scent towards them. They 'Get Wind' of the danger. Similarly, you 'get wind' of a Secret (राज) or rumor.
extras.origin: Hunting metaphor. Animals detect the presence of predators or hunters by the scent carried on the wind.

meanings.english: To prepare for hard work or a difficult task.

content.usage: We must gird up our loins for the difficult journey ahead.

extras.mnemonic: In ancient times, men wore long robes. Before Fighting or Running, they tied (Girded) the robe up between their legs (Loins) to move freely. It means Getting Ready (तैयार होना) for action.
extras.origin: Biblical origin. Men in the Near East wore tunics; 'girding the loins' meant tucking the tunic into the belt to free the legs for work, travel, or battle.

content.phrase: To go down like a lead balloon

meanings.english: A poorly received joke or idea; to fail completely.
meanings.hindi: बुरी तरह असफल होना (मजाक या प्रस्ताव का)।

content.usage: His joke went down like a lead balloon; nobody laughed.

extras.mnemonic: A Balloon floats because it is light. Lead (सीसा) is a heavy metal. A 'Lead Balloon' would crash to the floor instantly. It describes a Joke or Plan that Fails (असफल) heavily.
extras.origin: Mid-20th Century. An oxymoron used to describe failure. It gained fame when Keith Moon (The Who) allegedly suggested the name 'Led Zeppelin' based on the idea a band would go down like a lead balloon.

meanings.english: To arrive at an agreement through discussion; to create a plan with effort.

content.usage: They spent hours trying to hammer out a peace treaty.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A Blacksmith beating a lump of hot metal with a Hammer (हथोड़ा). It takes hard work to Shape it. 'Hammering out' a deal implies working hard to remove differences and Finalize (तय करना) it.
extras.origin: Blacksmithing metaphor. Just as a smith hammers metal into a desired shape, negotiators must hammer away at the details to forge an agreement.

meanings.english: To have a very good time.

content.usage: We had a blast at the concert last night.

extras.mnemonic: A Blast (धमाका) is exciting and loud. 'Having a blast' means having an explosively Good Time (मजे करना).
extras.origin: US slang (mid-20th century). Originally 'blast' meant a strong gust of wind or explosion. In jazz slang, it came to mean a great experience or party.

meanings.english: To go to sleep.

content.usage: I'm exhausted; I'm going to hit the sack.

extras.mnemonic: In old days, poor mattresses were just Sacks (बोरियां) filled with straw. When you lay down heavily on it, you 'Hit the Sack'. It means Going to Bed (सोने जाना).
extras.origin: American slang (late 19th/early 20th century). Refers to the material of mattresses (sacks stuffed with hay or straw).

meanings.english: To follow popular trends; to join a winning side.
meanings.hindi: बहती गंगा में हाथ धोना; देखा-देखी करना।

content.usage: Once the team started winning, everyone jumped on the bandwagon.

extras.mnemonic: A Bandwagon was a parade wagon carrying a musical band. Politicians used to ride them to attract crowds. People 'Jumped On' to be seen with the Popular (लोकप्रिय) winner. It means following the Crowd.
extras.origin: American politics (19th century). Bandwagons were used in parades. Politicians realized that riding the bandwagon attracted attention, so joining a successful cause became 'jumping on the bandwagon'.

meanings.english: In a state of suspension; temporarily inactive.
meanings.hindi: स्थगित रखना; कुछ समय के लिए रोक देना।

content.usage: The project was kept in abeyance until funding was secured.

extras.mnemonic: Abeyance means 'Waiting for an Owner' (Suspension/निलंबन). Think of a ball thrown in the air that hasn't landed yet. Keeping plans in abeyance means Pausing (रोकना) them for now.
extras.origin: Legal terminology from Old French 'abeance' (gaping/expecting). Originally referred to property or titles without a current owner, waiting to be claimed. Now means temporary suspension.

meanings.english: To be in touch; to maintain the same pace.

content.usage: It is hard to keep up with the fast pace of technology.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine running a race. If you 'Keep Up', you stay level with the leader. You don't fall behind. It means Maintaining the Pace (गति बनाए रखना) or staying updated.
extras.origin: Phrasal verb implies maintaining a position or pace relative to something else.

meanings.english: To make a lot of money.

content.usage: He made a pile investing in the stock market.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Stacking gold coins until you have a huge Pile (ढेर). 'Making a pile' literally means accumulating a Pile of Money (धन का ढेर).
extras.origin: Colloquial usage. 'Pile' has been slang for a large amount of money since the 18th century.

meanings.english: To stop something at the start before it grows.
meanings.hindi: पनपने से पहले ही नष्ट कर देना।

content.usage: Bad habits should be nipped in the bud.

extras.mnemonic: Gardening: If you see a weed Bud (कली), you pinch (Nip) it off immediately. If you wait, it grows into a big problem. 'Nip in the bud' means destroying a problem Early (जल्दी).
extras.origin: Horticultural metaphor. Referencing the practice of pinching off buds to prevent development. Used metaphorically since the 17th century for stopping plans or habits early.

meanings.english: To take revenge; to settle a grudge.

content.usage: He is just trying to pay off old scores rather than solve the problem.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Score' was a debt written on a tally stick. Paying off a score usually means clearing debt. But 'Old Scores' implies old grudges. Paying them off means taking Revenge (बदला).
extras.origin: From the practice of keeping accounts (scores) on tally sticks or slate. 'Settling a score' meant paying a debt, later evolving to mean settling a grudge or grievance.

meanings.english: To criticise someone; to find faults.

content.usage: It is easy to pick holes in someone else's work.

extras.mnemonic: Think of inspecting a piece of cloth. You pull and 'Pick' at it, trying to find or create a small Hole (छेद/कमी). It means looking for Faults (कमियां) intentionally.
extras.origin: Textile metaphor. Refers to picking at fabric to find flaws or holes. Metaphorically, it means scrutinizing an argument or work to find minor faults.

meanings.english: To restart from the previous closing point; to resume.
meanings.hindi: फिर से शुरू करना (जहां से छोड़ा था)।

content.usage: After the interruption, he tried to pick up the threads of his story.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A weaver stops working. The Threads (धागे) are loose. To start again, he must 'Pick Up' the loose ends and connect them. It means Resuming (फिर शुरू करना) a story or life after a break.
extras.origin: Weaving/Spinning metaphor. If a thread breaks or work is paused, the spinner must pick up the thread ends to continue the pattern. Applies to resuming a story or relationship.

meanings.english: To unfairly categorize someone; to put aside for later.
meanings.hindi: खांचे में बांटना (सीमित करना); ठंडे बस्ते में डालना।

content.usage: Don't pigeon hole him as just a comedian; he is a great actor too.

extras.mnemonic: Old desks had small square boxes for sorting mail, looking like a Pigeon Coop (Pigeon Hole). Putting a document there meant filing it away or Categorizing (श्रेणीबद्ध करना) it strictly.
extras.origin: Refers to the compartments in old desks or post offices used for sorting papers. Figuratively, it means to classify someone too rigidly or to put a matter aside (file it away).

meanings.english: To seek to win approval from the crowd; to perform for popularity.
meanings.hindi: जनता को खुश करने के लिए काम करना; वाहवाही लूटना।

content.usage: The politician was accusing of playing to the gallery instead of addressing real issues.

extras.mnemonic: In a theater, the 'Gallery' was the highest, cheapest balcony where the common crowd sat. Actors who made cheap jokes to please this noisy crowd were 'Playing to the Gallery' for easy Applause (वाहवाही).
extras.origin: Theatrical term. The gallery housed the cheapest seats and the most boisterous audience. Acting in a way to solicit their cheers (overacting/pandering) was considered low-brow.

content.phrase: To put one out of countenance

meanings.english: To make one feel ashamed or disconcerted.

content.usage: The staring of the crowd put him out of countenance.

extras.mnemonic: Countenance means 'Face' or 'Expression'. If you are calm, you keep your countenance. If someone stares at you and makes you lose your confidence, you are 'Put Out' of your calm face. You feel Ashamed (शर्मिंदा).
extras.origin: 16th Century. 'Countenance' refers to the look or expression of the face (composure). To put someone out of countenance is to disturb their composure or make them lose face.

meanings.english: Tolerate; to endure someone or something unpleasant.

content.usage: I cannot put up with his rude behavior anymore.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine an annoying guest comes to your house. You have to 'Put them Up' (provide a room/bed). Even if they are rude, you must Endure (बर्दाश्त करना) them while they stay.
extras.origin: Phrasal verb usage. Originally 'to put up' meant to house or lodge someone. 'Putting up with' implies bearing the burden or inconvenience of their presence.

meanings.english: To run about in a frenzy; to behave uncontrollably.
meanings.hindi: आपा खोकर इधर-उधर भागना; उपद्रव मचाना।

content.usage: The cattle ran amok in the market, overturning stalls.

extras.mnemonic: Derived from a Malay word for a warrior who gets so angry he attacks everyone. Visual: A mad bull Running (भागना) wildly in the streets, destroying everything in a Frenzy (पागलपन).
extras.origin: From the Malay word 'amuk', describing a state of murderous frenzy. It entered English via travelers in the 17th century to describe warriors who would attack indiscriminately.

meanings.english: To have a casual conversation; to chat aimlessly.
meanings.hindi: गपशप करना; हवा में बातें करना।

content.usage: We sat on the porch and shot the breeze for hours.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine sitting with a gun Shooting at the Wind (Breeze/हवा). You hit nothing. It is a useless activity, just like idle Chatting (गपशप) that accomplishes nothing serious.
extras.origin: American slang (early 20th century). Similar to 'chewing the fat'. Shooting at the breeze implies wasting time or ammunition on nothing substantial.

meanings.english: To avoid deliberately; to reject.

content.usage: After the scandal, he was shunned by his former friends.

extras.mnemonic: Shun sounds like Sun (सूरज). Vampires shun the Sun because it hurts them. If you 'shun' a person, you stay away from them as if they are dangerous or unwanted. You Avoid (बचना) them.
extras.origin: From Old English 'scunian' meaning to abhor or avoid. It implies a deliberate and often moralistic decision to keep away from something.

meanings.english: To be dead (usually murdered and body disposed of in water).

content.usage: If you cross the mafia, you might end up sleeping with the fishes.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A gangster throws a body into the ocean with cement shoes. The body sinks to the bottom and 'Sleeps' (rests) there, surrounded by Fishes (मछलियाँ).
extras.origin: Popularized by the movie 'The Godfather' (1972). It is a translation of an old Sicilian Mafia phrase indicating a rival has been killed and thrown into the sea.

meanings.english: To act foolishly; to take action that leads to inevitable trouble/war.

content.usage: By ignoring the grievances of the people, the government is sowing the dragon's teeth.

extras.mnemonic: Myth: Cadmus killed a dragon and planted (sowed) its Teeth (दांत) in the ground. Instead of plants, armed Soldiers grew out of the dirt and started fighting. Planting evil seeds creates Conflict (संघर्ष).
extras.origin: Greek Mythology. In the stories of Cadmus and Jason, sowing the teeth of a dragon resulted in a crop of fierce warriors who fought to the death. It metaphorically means doing something that creates future strife.

meanings.english: Postpone; to avert or delay something bad.

content.usage: He ate a snack to stave off hunger until dinner.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A pack of wolves attacking. You use a strong wooden Staff/Stick (Stave/डंडा) to push them back and keep them away. You are 'Staving Off' the Attack (हमला रोकना).
extras.origin: 17th Century. To 'stave' meant to break the staves (wooden slats) of a barrel. However, 'stave off' likely comes from using a staff or stave to fend off an attacking animal.

meanings.english: To outshine; to gain an advantage secretly or unexpectedly.
meanings.hindi: चुपके से बाजी मार ले जाना; दूसरों से आगे निकल जाना।

content.usage: Our competitor stole a march on us by launching their product a week early.

extras.mnemonic: War strategy: While the enemy sleeps, you secretly March (कूच करना) your army at night. By morning, you are ahead of them. You 'stole' the advantage of time/distance. It means getting Ahead (आगे) secretly.
extras.origin: Military tactic. Moving troops secretly (often at night) to gain a positional advantage over the enemy before they are aware.

meanings.english: Avoid; to keep a distance from.

content.usage: You should steer clear of bad company.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Driving a boat (Steering/जहाज चलाना). You see dangerous Rocks ahead. You turn the wheel to stay in Clear water (साफ पानी) and Avoid (बचना) the crash.
extras.origin: Nautical terminology. To steer a ship so that it stays 'clear' (at a safe distance) from obstructions like reefs or other vessels.

meanings.english: To be the most remarkable (often used ironically for something very bad or stupid).
meanings.hindi: हद कर देना (अक्सर नकारात्मक अर्थ में)।

content.usage: I've heard some dumb excuses, but that one takes the cake.

extras.mnemonic: In old dance contests, the winner was given a Cake (केक) as a prize. So 'Taking the Cake' means Winning (जीतना) or being the 'most' extreme example of something (even stupidity).
extras.origin: Refers to the 'cakewalk' competitions among African Americans in the 19th century, where the most graceful walkers won a cake as a prize.

content.phrase: To take someone for a ride

meanings.english: To deceive someone; to cheat.

content.usage: I paid for a genuine leather jacket, but I think I was taken for a ride.

extras.mnemonic: Gangster movies: They invite a victim for a 'Ride' in the car, drive to a lonely spot, and Rob or Kill him. Taking someone for a ride means Deceiving (धोखा देना) or cheating them.
extras.origin: American underworld slang (1920s). A 'one-way ride' meant kidnapping someone to murder them. It softened to general deception or swindling.

meanings.english: To scold someone; to criticize/reprimand.

content.usage: The teacher took him to task for not completing his homework.

extras.mnemonic: If you are lazy, the boss gives you a difficult Task (काम) as a punishment. 'Taking you to task' means forcing you to explain your failure or Scolding (डांटना) you for it.
extras.origin: 16th Century. Originally meant 'to require someone to give an account' or to assign a specific burden of labor as correction.

meanings.english: In every detail; exactly.

content.usage: He followed the instructions to the letter.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Reading a contract. You follow every single Letter (अक्षर) A, B, C... You don't skip anything. It means following instructions Exactly (बिल्कुल वैसे ही).
extras.origin: Derived from 'to the last letter', implying attention to the smallest detail of the written word.

meanings.english: Express extreme anger; to have a tantrum.

content.usage: The toddler threw a fit when he couldn't get the candy.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Fit' is a medical Seizure (दौरा) where the body shakes uncontrollably. If someone 'throws a fit', they act so Angry (गुस्सा) that they look like they are having a seizure.
extras.origin: Medical metaphor. 'Fit' refers to a sudden attack of illness (like epilepsy). Figuratively, it describes a sudden, uncontrollable outburst of emotion.

meanings.english: To follow the lead; to obey rules strictly.
meanings.hindi: नियमों का पालन करना; आदेश मानना।

content.usage: In the army, you have to toe the line or face punishment.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Soldiers or runners standing in a row. They must place the tip of their shoe (Toe/अंगूठा) exactly on the white Line. If they cross it, they break the rules. It means Obeying (आज्ञा पालन) strictly.
extras.origin: Disputed. Could be from the Navy (sailors lining up for inspection), prizefighting (fighters standing at a line to start), or track sports. All involve standing strictly at a marked line.

content.phrase: To turn over a new leaf

meanings.english: To change one's behaviour for the better.
meanings.hindi: नया जीवन शुरू करना; सुधर जाना।

content.usage: After getting out of prison, he promised to turn over a new leaf.

extras.mnemonic: In old English, a page of a book was called a 'Leaf' (पन्ना). Turning over a new leaf means turning the page to start a New Chapter (नया अध्याय) in life.
extras.origin: 16th Century. Refers to turning the page of a book to start a fresh lesson or chapter, symbolizing a fresh start.

meanings.english: In an ironic way; not seriously.

content.usage: His comment was meant tongue in cheek, so don't get offended.

extras.mnemonic: Try pushing your Tongue (जीभ) into your Cheek (गाल). It makes a funny face. People did this to stop themselves from laughing while telling a Lie or Joke. It means Insincere (मजाक).
extras.origin: 18th Century. Originally an act of contempt, later evolved to a facial gesture used to suppress laughter or indicate that a statement is not to be taken seriously.

meanings.english: Uncertain; precarious situation.
meanings.hindi: अनिश्चित; जो कभी भी बिगड़ सकता है।

content.usage: The surgery was touch and go, but he survived.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a ship in shallow water. It Touches (छूना) the bottom sand but doesn't get stuck—it Goes (चलना) on. It was a Risky (जोखिम) moment where it almost crashed. It means a very uncertain situation.
extras.origin: Nautical term. Refers to a ship grazing the seabed (touching) but continuing to move (going) without getting stuck. It implies a delicate and risky situation.

meanings.english: Turn upside down (usually a boat or car).

content.usage: The car skidded and turned turtle on the highway.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A Turtle (कछुआ) flipped on its back. Its legs wave in the air, and it is Helpless (असहाय). If a car or boat 'turns turtle', it has flipped Upside Down (उल्टा).
extras.origin: Nautical term. Sailors captured sea turtles by flipping them onto their backs, rendering them helpless. Applied to boats capsizing in the same manner.

meanings.english: Appear; arrive.

content.usage: He promised to come, but he didn't turn up.

extras.mnemonic: When something is lost, you hope it will 'Turn Up' (प्रकट होना) eventually. Similarly, if a person 'turns up', they Arrive (पहुंचना) at the location.
extras.origin: Phrasal verb. Possibly related to turning up a playing card (revealing it) or digging/ploughing (turning up soil to find things).

meanings.english: To be angry; protesting vigorously.
meanings.hindi: विद्रोह पर उतारू; कड़ा विरोध करना।

content.usage: The residents were up in arms against the new construction project.

extras.mnemonic: Arms here means Weapons (हथियार). When people are 'Up in Arms', they have picked up their guns/swords to Fight (लड़ना) or protest violently.
extras.origin: Military term. Literally meant rising up ready for battle, with weapons (arms) in hand. Now used metaphorically for strong protest.

meanings.english: Face to face; in relation to; compared with.

content.usage: We need to discuss our strategy vis-à-vis the competition.

extras.mnemonic: French phrase. Visage means Face (चेहरा). Vis-à-vis literally means 'Face to Face'. It implies looking at something closely to Compare (तुलना करना) it or see the relationship.
extras.origin: French loanword adopted in the 18th century. Literally 'face to face'. Originally referred to a carriage where passengers sat facing each other.

meanings.english: To divert attention from something important (a small part controlling the whole).
meanings.hindi: ध्यान भटकाना; छोटी चीज का बड़ी चीज को नियंत्रित करना।

content.usage: The government started a war to wag the dog and distract from the scandal.

extras.mnemonic: Usually, the Dog is big and the Tail is small. The dog wags the tail. If the Tail Wags the Dog (पूंछ कुत्ते को हिलाए), the small part is controlling the big part. It means creating a distraction to Control (नियंत्रण) the situation.
extras.origin: From an old joke: 'Why does a dog wag its tail? Because the dog is smarter. If the tail were smarter, it would wag the dog.' Popularized by the movie 'Wag the Dog' (1997) involving political distraction.

meanings.english: No longer important; past events that cannot be changed.
meanings.hindi: जो बीत गई सो बात गई; पुरानी बात।

content.usage: We had arguments in the past, but that is all water under the bridge now.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A river flowing under a bridge. The water that has passed is Gone (चला गया). It can never come back. Past mistakes are like that water—Past is Past (बीत गया).
extras.origin: Standard idiom referencing the unidirectional flow of a river. Once water flows past a point, it cannot return, symbolizing the irreversibility of time and past events.

meanings.english: Good and bad times; prosperity and misfortune.

content.usage: They promised to stay together through weal and woe.

extras.mnemonic: Old English words: 'Weal' means Wealth/Happiness (सुख). 'Woe' means Sadness (दुख). 'Weal and Woe' is the rhyming version of 'Ups and Downs'.
extras.origin: Old English. 'Weal' (wela) meant wealth or well-being. 'Woe' (wa) meant misery. The pairing is a traditional alliterative phrase for the vicissitudes of life.

meanings.english: Suffer unrequited love; to mourn a lost love.
meanings.hindi: वियोग में होना; प्यार में धोखा खाना।

content.usage: He has been wearing the green willow since she rejected his proposal.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Weeping Willow' tree has drooping branches that look like tears falling. In old stories, rejected lovers wore a wreath of Willow (विलो) branches to show their Sadness (गम).
extras.origin: Shakespearean/Folklore imagery. The willow tree is traditionally associated with grief and unrequited love (e.g., Ophelia in Hamlet, Desdemona's song in Othello).

meanings.english: To prepare a ship to sail; to lift the anchor.

content.usage: We weigh anchor at dawn.

extras.mnemonic: Not about checking weight! 'Weigh' here means to Lift/Heave. 'Weighing Anchor' means pulling the heavy Anchor (लंगर) up from the sea bottom so the ship can Move (चलना).
extras.origin: Nautical term. 'Weigh' comes from the Old English 'wegan', meaning to move or carry. The anchor is 'weighed' (lifted) off the seabed to allow the ship to sail.

meanings.english: To get drunk; to have a drink.

content.usage: He stopped at the pub to wet his whistle.

extras.mnemonic: If you try to whistle with a dry mouth, no sound comes out. You must Wet (गीला करना) your lips and throat. 'Whistle' here is slang for Throat/Mouth (गला). It means Taking a Drink.
extras.origin: Chaucerian English (The Canterbury Tales). 'Whistle' meant throat or voice. Wetting it cleared the voice. It persisted as a colloquial term for having a drink.

meanings.english: What a coincidence (meeting someone unexpectedly).

content.usage: I met my old school friend in Paris—what a small world!

extras.mnemonic: The world is huge. But if you meet your neighbor in a foreign country, it feels like the world has Shrunk (सिकुड़ना). It is a Small World (छोटी दुनिया) because everyone is connected.
extras.origin: Common expression emerging from the 20th century as travel became easier, making unlikely meetings more common.

meanings.english: Never going to happen; impossible.

content.usage: He will pay you back when pigs fly.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A fat Pig flapping wings in the sky. Impossible, right? Pigs are heavy and have no wings. 'When pigs fly' means Never (कभी नहीं).
extras.origin: Adynaton (a figure of speech for impossibility). A version 'pigs might fly' has been used since the 1600s (originally 'snails may fly'), to mock unrealistic hopes.

meanings.english: Pretend to be unafraid; to put on a brave front.

content.usage: He says he isn't worried about the exam, but I think he is just whistling in the dark.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A child walking alone in the Dark (अंधेरा). He is scared of ghosts, so he Whistles (सीटी बजाना) a tune to distract himself and Pretend (नाटक) he is brave.
extras.origin: Observed behavior. People often make noise (humming/whistling) when frightened in solitary, dark places to comfort themselves and ward off fear.

meanings.english: Enjoy in a noisy way; to celebrate boisterously.

content.usage: The team went to whoop it up after winning the championship.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Whoop' is a loud cry of excitement (Woohoo!). 'Whooping it up' means making loud noise, partying, and celebrating Wildly (हल्ला-गुल्ला).
extras.origin: 19th Century American slang (Cowboy era). Refers to the loud 'whooping' calls made during boisterous celebrations or drunken sprees.

meanings.english: Impossible to achieve; a deceptive goal or hope.

content.usage: Chasing perfection is a will-o-the-wisp.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Wisp' is a bundle of burning straw (Torch). In folklore, a ghostly light (Will) floats over swamps. Travelers follow it hoping for shelter but get lost and drown. It is a Deception (छलावा).
extras.origin: Folklore. Refers to atmospheric ghost lights (ignis fatuus) seen over marshes. Legend says a spirit (Will) with a lantern (wisp) lures travelers to their doom. Metaphor for a misleading hope.

meanings.english: To cheat/rob or defeat someone.

content.usage: The con artist wiped the nose of the tourist. (Note: Similar to 'Wipe someone's eye').

extras.mnemonic: To treat someone like a helpless child whose Nose (नाक) needs wiping. Or to blind them momentarily to Steal (चोरी) from them. It implies taking advantage of someone.
extras.origin: Old slang usage. Similar to 'wiping someone's eye' (shooting game they intended to shoot). It implies beating someone to the prize or cheating them.

meanings.english: Carefully; with great attention to detail.

content.usage: The police searched the house with a fine-tooth comb.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Fine-tooth comb' has teeth very close together, used to catch tiny Lice (जूँ) in hair. Searching with one means checking every tiny strand. It implies a very Thorough Search (बारीकी से जांच).
extras.origin: Hygiene metaphor. Combs with closely spaced teeth were used to remove parasites/lice. To 'go over' something with such a comb meant missing absolutely nothing.

meanings.english: Excellent service; efficient and useful help in need.

content.usage: The volunteers did yeoman's service during the flood relief.

extras.mnemonic: A Yeoman was a middle-class farmer who also served as a loyal Soldier/Guard. They were known for being Hardworking (मेहनती) and Reliable. 'Yeoman's service' is excellent, reliable help.
extras.origin: Medieval English social structure. Yeomen were freeholders below the gentry who formed the backbone of the English army (e.g., longbowmen). Their service was considered sturdy and reliable.

meanings.english: Be very poor; living at subsistence level.

content.usage: Many families are living on the breadline due to unemployment.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Poor people standing in a long Line (कतार) to get free Bread (रोटी) from a charity. Being 'on the breadline' means you are so Poor (गरीब) you rely on handouts to eat.
extras.origin: 1860s America/Great Depression. In times of economic crisis, charities or bakeries formed lines to distribute free or cheap bread to the starving unemployed.

meanings.english: To narrowly avoid a situation or disaster.

content.usage: I dodged a bullet by not investing in that company before it crashed.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A soldier on the battlefield. A bullet flies at his head, but he ducks (Dodges) just in time. He Survives (बचना). It implies escaping a disaster narrowly.
extras.origin: Military/Combat metaphor. Literally avoiding gunfire. Figuratively, avoiding a detrimental relationship, bad investment, or disastrous event.

meanings.english: Smells foul; a bad smell.

content.usage: There was a terrible pen and ink coming from the garbage bin.

extras.mnemonic: Cockney Rhyming Slang: 'Pen and Ink' rhymes with Stink (बदबू). So, if something is 'pen and ink', it Stinks.
extras.origin: Cockney Rhyming Slang (London). A code where a phrase rhymes with the intended word. 'Pen and Ink' = Stink.

meanings.english: To make someone laugh.

content.usage: His jokes always crack me up.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A stone face that suddenly 'Cracks' (टूटन) into a Smile. If you 'crack up', your composure breaks and Laughter (हंसी) bursts out.
extras.origin: Etymology relates to breaking or bursting ('cracking a smile'). Originally meant to break down physically/mentally, but in modern slang, it refers to bursting into laughter.

meanings.english: An easy and pleasant job; a highly desirable position.
meanings.hindi: मलाईदार नौकरी; आसान और अच्छी नौकरी।

content.usage: He landed a plum job at the embassy.

extras.mnemonic: A Plum (आलूबुखारा) is a sweet, juicy fruit. In old slang, 'Plum' meant a large sum of Money (पैसा). A 'plum job' is sweet, easy, and pays well.
extras.origin: 17th Century. 'Plum' was slang for £100,000 or a wealthy person. It evolved to describe anything desirable, profitable, or 'sweet' (like the fruit).

meanings.english: Made a mess; botched something up.

content.usage: He tried to fix the sink but made a pig's ear of it.

extras.mnemonic: A Pig's Ear (सूअर का कान) is floppy, dirty, and hairy. You cannot turn it into a 'silk purse' (something valuable). Making a 'pig's ear' of something means creating a Mess (गड़बड़).
extras.origin: Mid-20th Century British slang. Likely derived from the proverb 'You can't make a silk purse out of a sow's ear', implying a botched attempt to make something good.

meanings.english: Burst of energy after tiring.
meanings.hindi: नई ऊर्जा; पुनः शक्ति प्राप्त करना।

content.usage: I was tired at mile 10, but then I got my second wind and finished the marathon.

extras.mnemonic: Think of running. First you run out of breath (Wind). You feel dead. Suddenly, your body adjusts and you get a 'Second Wind' (दुसरी सांस). You feel Energetic (ऊर्जावान) again.
extras.origin: Sports/Running phenomenon. Refers to the point during prolonged exercise where breathlessness subsides and the athlete feels a renewed surge of energy.

meanings.english: Provide useful advantage; everything can be used for profit.
meanings.hindi: फायदे का सौदा; काम की चीज।

content.usage: Every experience is grist to the mill for a writer.

extras.mnemonic: A Mill grinds grain into flour. 'Grist' is the Grain (अनाज) brought to the mill. For the miller, any grain is good because it makes flour. It means anything can be turned to Profit (फायदा).
extras.origin: 16th Century milling proverb: 'All is grist that comes to his mill'. It means everything received can be utilized for gain.

meanings.english: Materialistic wealth or benefits.

content.usage: He is only interested in the loaves and fishes of the office, not the work itself.

extras.mnemonic: Bible Story: Jesus fed a crowd with Loaves (bread) and Fishes. Some people followed him only for the Free Food (Material Reward/लाभ), not his teachings. It means seeking personal profit.
extras.origin: Biblical (John 6:26). Jesus rebuked the multitude for following him not because of the miracles, but because they ate the loaves and were filled. It symbolizes material gain.

meanings.english: Exactly; perfectly.

content.usage: The suit fits him to a T.

extras.mnemonic: A 'T-Square' is a ruler used by draftsmen to draw Perfect (सटीक) straight lines and right angles. If something fits 'to a T', it fits perfectly like a precise drawing.
extras.origin: Uncertain. Likely refers to the T-square used in drawing/carpentry for precision, or possibly 'to a tittle' (the small dot over an 'i'), meaning to the smallest detail.

meanings.english: To tell others what to do; to be in control.

content.usage: Now that she is the manager, she calls the shots.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A Director or Commander. They shout the order when to Shoot (गोली चलाना). The person who gives the order is the Boss (मालिक).
extras.origin: Target shooting. A signalman calls out where the shots have hit the target to help the marksman adjust. Also implies military command.

meanings.english: A life filled with excitement and activity.

content.usage: Living in the fast lane can be exhausting after a while.

extras.mnemonic: On a highway, the 'Fast Lane' is for high-speed cars. Living 'in the fast lane' means living a High Speed (तेज) life full of parties, risk, and excitement.
extras.origin: Driving metaphor. The overtaking lane on a motorway is the 'fast lane'. Metaphorically applied to a lifestyle that is high-paced, risky, and affluent.

meanings.english: Smart and clever; cunning.

content.usage: He deceived everyone; he is as sly as a fox.

extras.mnemonic: In every fable, the Fox (लोमड़ी) tricks other animals to steal food. Foxes are symbols of Cleverness (चालाकी) and deception.
extras.origin: From traditional fables (Aesop) and folklore where the fox is characteristically portrayed as the trickster—crafty, intelligent, and deceitful.

meanings.english: Completely different from each other.

content.usage: The two brothers are like chalk and cheese.

extras.mnemonic: Chalk is white, dry, and inedible. Cheese can look white but is soft and tasty. They might look similar from a distance, but they are Totally Different (बिल्कुल अलग) in nature.
extras.origin: 14th Century proverb. It emphasizes that surface similarities (both can be white/pale solids) do not equate to substance. They are fundamentally opposites.

content.phrase: As phony as a 3 dollar bill

meanings.english: Something that is fake; not genuine.

content.usage: His apology was as phony as a three-dollar bill.

extras.mnemonic: Have you ever seen a $3 Bill (नोट)? No, because the government never printed one. If someone gives you a $3 bill, it is 100% Fake (नकली).
extras.origin: American colloquialism. Since no three-dollar bill has ever been in legal circulation in the US, the phrase is used to describe something blatantly counterfeit or false.

meanings.english: To look foolish; to be embarrassed because of a mistake.

content.usage: He had egg on his face after the project he championed failed miserably.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine performing on stage. The audience hates you and throws raw Eggs (अंडे). You stand there with sticky yellow egg dripping down your face. You look Foolish (मूर्ख) and embarrassed.
extras.origin: Uncertain, possibly from theater audiences throwing rotten eggs at bad actors, or from breaking an egg while eating (messy eater). It signifies public embarrassment.

meanings.english: Desperate attempt to succeed; trying anything to find a solution.
meanings.hindi: मरता क्या न करता (तिनके का सहारा ढूंढना)।

content.usage: The police are just grasping at straws; they have no real evidence.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A man Drowning in a river. He is so desperate he tries to grab floating Straws (तिनके) to save himself, even though straw cannot hold his weight. It represents Desperation (हताशा).
extras.origin: From the proverb 'A drowning man will catch at a straw'. It illustrates that in a desperate situation, a person will try any option, no matter how futile it seems.

meanings.english: Average; ordinary; not special.

content.usage: It was just a run-of-the-mill movie, nothing exciting.

extras.mnemonic: A Mill produces thousands of identical items (bricks, cloth). The 'Run' (production batch) from the mill is standard and Ordinary (साधारण). It is not custom-made or special.
extras.origin: Manufacturing terminology. Refers to goods produced by a mill that are not graded or sorted for quality—just the average, standard output.

meanings.english: To use one's last resource; to accept the poorest option because nothing else is left.
meanings.hindi: बचा-खुचा इस्तेमाल करना (क्योंकि और कुछ नहीं है)।

content.usage: They really scraped the barrel to find a candidate for the election.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a Barrel of apples. The best ones are on top. When they are gone, you have to 'Scrape the Bottom' to get the dirty, bruised apples left. It means choosing the Worst (खराब) option because you have no choice.
extras.origin: Food storage metaphor. In the days of food barrels (pork/meal), the items at the bottom were the oldest and worst quality. Reaching them meant supplies were exhausted.

meanings.english: Bad luck.

content.usage: It was just bad iron that he got sick on the day of the interview.

extras.mnemonic: Iron is hard and unyielding. In Australian slang, 'Bad Iron' refers to a bad toss of a coin or a bad lie of a golf ball. It is a situation of Tough Luck (दुर्भाग्य).
extras.origin: Australian slang. Likely derived from the sensation of striking a 'bad iron' (poor tool/weapon) or bad luck in shearing/gambling contexts.

meanings.english: Doing a job quickly; alert and efficient.

content.usage: The new assistant is really on the ball; she anticipates problems before they happen.

extras.mnemonic: In sports like Tennis or Cricket, you must keep your eye 'On the Ball' (गेंद पर नजर). If you are alert, you react fast. Being 'on the ball' means being Alert (सतर्क) and competent.
extras.origin: Sports terminology (Baseball/Cricket/Football). A player who is 'on the ball' is attentive and ready to play, anticipating the movement of the game.

meanings.english: Completely dead; obsolete; out of trend.
meanings.hindi: पूरी तरह से बेजान; जिसका अस्तित्व खत्म हो चुका हो।

content.usage: That old fashion trend is as dead as a doornail.

extras.mnemonic: In old doors, nails were hammered in and then the tip was bent over (clinched) so they couldn't be pulled out. These nails were 'Dead' (useless for reuse). It means Totally Dead (पूरी तरह मृत).
extras.origin: 14th Century. Used by Langland and Shakespeare. Refers to a nail that is clinched (bent) into the wood, making it immovable and 'dead' for any other purpose.

meanings.english: An unreal hope; an impossible plan.

content.usage: His plan to become a billionaire overnight is just a pipe dream.

extras.mnemonic: Refers to an Opium Pipe. When people smoked opium, they had wonderful, magical hallucinations (Dreams). But they were fake. A 'pipe dream' is a Fantasy (कल्पना) that will never happen.
extras.origin: Late 19th Century. Refers to the hallucinations or fantasies induced by smoking an opium pipe. It implies a plan or hope that is as unrealistic as a drug-induced dream.

meanings.english: Obey someone's every wish; to follow someone around seeking to please them.

content.usage: I refuse to dance attendance on him just because he is rich.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A servant 'Dancing' around a King, waiting for orders. He moves constantly to stay close and Serve (सेवा करना). It implies servile, flattering behavior.
extras.origin: 16th Century. Originally described the custom of waiting (attending) at a wedding where the bride was danced with. It evolved to mean waiting on someone hand and foot, often obsequiously.

meanings.english: To buy something worthless (usually a car that breaks down constantly).

content.usage: I realized too late that the used car I bought was a lemon.

extras.mnemonic: Lemons are Sour (खट्टा). If you think you bought a sweet orange (a good car) but bite into a sour lemon (a broken car), you are disappointed. A 'lemon' is a Defective Product (खराब माल).
extras.origin: American slang (early 1900s). 'Lemon' meant a loser or something undesirable/sour. By the 1920s, it specifically referred to a defective car.

meanings.english: Starting to perform very well; functioning smoothly.
meanings.hindi: लय में होना; बेहतरीन प्रदर्शन करना।

content.usage: After a slow start, the team got in the groove and won the match.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Vinyl Record player. The music sounds perfect only when the needle sits securely in the Groove (खांचा). Being 'in the groove' means performing Smoothly (सुचारू रूप से) and perfectly.
extras.origin: Jazz slang (1920s). Musicians used it to describe playing effortlessly and perfectly, 'in the pocket' or 'in the groove' of the rhythm.

meanings.english: Similar thing/person; an exact duplicate.

content.usage: He is a dead ringer for his father.

extras.mnemonic: Horse Racing: A 'Ringer' is a fast horse secretly swapped for a slow one to cheat. 'Dead' means Exact. A 'Dead Ringer' is a duplicate so perfect it tricks you. Exact Copy (हूबहू नकल).
extras.origin: Horse racing slang (19th century). A 'ringer' was a horse substituted for another of similar appearance to defraud bookies. 'Dead' is used as an intensifier meaning 'exact' or 'absolute'.

meanings.english: Made an irreversible change/decision.
meanings.hindi: निर्णायक कदम उठाना (जिससे वापसी संभव न हो)।

content.usage: By resigning from his job, he has crossed the Rubicon.

extras.mnemonic: History: Julius Caesar arrived at the River Rubicon. By Crossing it with his army, he declared war on Rome. He could not go back. It means reaching the Point of No Return (वापसी का कोई रास्ता नहीं).
extras.origin: Roman History (49 BC). Julius Caesar crossed the Rubicon river, breaking the law that forbade generals from leading armies into Italy. It marked the start of civil war and irrevocable action.

meanings.english: To become excited or angry; to go crazy.
meanings.hindi: पागल हो जाना (खुशी या गुस्से में)।
