content.usage: The kids went bananas when I told them we were going to Disneyland.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A Monkey seeing a pile of Bananas (केले). It screams, jumps, and goes Crazy (पागल). 'Going bananas' means acting wildly excited or angry.
extras.origin: 1960s American slang. Likely derived from the stereotype of monkeys going into a frenzy of excitement at the sight of bananas.

meanings.english: An old vehicle (usually a car or ship) that is in bad condition.

content.usage: He drives an old rust bucket that makes a lot of noise.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A metal Bucket that is so Old that it is full of Rust (जंग). It might leak. Calling a car a 'rust bucket' means it is old, rusty, and ready for the Scrap (कबाड़).
extras.origin: Originally naval slang (mid-20th century) for an old, rusty destroyer or ship. Later applied to cars in poor condition.

meanings.english: To go to bed.

content.usage: It's late; I'm going to hit the hay.

extras.mnemonic: Before foam mattresses, beds were sacks stuffed with dried Hay (सूखी घास). When you lie down heavily, you 'Hit the Hay'. It means Going to Sleep (सोने जाना).
extras.origin: Early 20th Century. Refers to mattresses stuffed with hay or straw. Similar to 'hit the sack'.

meanings.english: An energetic person; a fine fellow.

content.usage: He's a real broth of a boy, always ready to help.

extras.mnemonic: Broth is a rich, nutritious Soup (शोरबा). It gives strength. A 'Broth of a Boy' is a boy who is full of Substance, energy, and Goodness (अच्छाई). He is a fine lad.
extras.origin: Irish idiom. 'Broth' implies the essence or best part of something. A 'broth of a boy' is the essence of what a boy should be—spirited and lively.

meanings.english: To gossip or chat casually.

content.usage: We sat around chewing the fat about old times.

extras.mnemonic: In old days, sailors ate salted pork Fat (चर्बी). It was tough and took hours to Chew (चबाना). While chewing, they talked. So 'chewing the fat' means Talking for a long time (लंबी बातचीत).
extras.origin: Nautical/Military slang. Chewing on tough salt pork was a leisure activity accompanied by conversation. Alternatively, it refers to the jaw movement of talking resembling chewing.

meanings.english: Keep sharing the latest information; keep updated.

content.usage: Keep me posted on the status of the project.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A notice board. You 'Post' (chipkana) a new notice to Inform people. 'Keep me posted' means keep sending me the News (खबर) like putting updates on a board.
extras.origin: Derived from the system of 'posting' letters or notices. Keeping someone posted meant ensuring the latest news was sent to them via post or displayed for them.

meanings.english: To annoy or upset someone.

content.usage: His arrogant comments really ruffled the feathers of the board members.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Bird. When it is calm, its feathers are flat. When it gets Angry (गुस्सा) or fights, it Ruffles (fluffs up) its Feathers (पंख). Doing this to a person means Annoying them.
extras.origin: Bird behavior metaphor. Birds fluff or ruffle their feathers when agitated, angry, or fighting. Applied to humans, it means disturbing their composure.

meanings.english: I am not convinced; I don't believe it.
meanings.hindi: मैं नहीं मानता; मुझे विश्वास नहीं है।

content.usage: He says he was sick, but I don't buy it.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine an idea is a Product for sale. If you believe it is true, you 'Buy' it. If you think it is a Lie (झूठ), you refuse to pay. 'I don't buy it' means 'I don't believe it'.
extras.origin: Commercial metaphor (mid-20th century). Accepting an idea or excuse is equated to purchasing goods. Refusing to 'buy' means rejecting the validity of the statement.

meanings.english: A mild punishment.

content.usage: He got off with just a slap on the wrist for speeding.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A child steals a cookie. The mother gently Slaps his Wrist (कलाई) and says 'No'. It hurts a little but isn't serious. It represents a very Light Punishment (हल्की सजा).
extras.origin: 18th Century. A metaphor for a punishment that is lenient or merely symbolic, causing no real harm, like hitting a hand lightly instead of a severe beating.

meanings.english: Market where prices are rising.

content.usage: Investors make more money during a bull market.

extras.mnemonic: How does a Bull fight? It thrusts its horns Upwards (ऊपर). So a 'Bull Market' is when prices go UP. (Contrast with Bear, which swipes Down).
extras.origin: Stock market terminology. Origin disputed, but commonly linked to the way a bull attacks (thrusting horns up) representing rising prices, vs a bear (swiping paws down) representing falling prices.

meanings.english: To be badly defeated; to meet one's final obstacle.

content.usage: After years of success, the politician met his Waterloo in the recent election.

extras.mnemonic: Napoleon was a great conqueror, but he was finally Defeated (हारा) at the Battle of Waterloo. Meeting your 'Waterloo' means facing a defeat you cannot recover from.
extras.origin: Historical. Refers to the Battle of Waterloo (1815), where Napoleon Bonaparte suffered his final and decisive defeat, ending his rule as Emperor of the French.

meanings.english: To ease pressure on something; to be lazy.

content.usage: Don't slack off now; we are almost finished.

extras.mnemonic: A rope that is tight is working. A rope that is 'Slack' (ढीला) is loose and doing nothing. To 'slack off' means to stop working hard and become Lazy (आलसी).
extras.origin: Nautical origin. A slack rope bears no load. 'Slacking off' meant loosening a rope. Applied to people, it means reducing effort or diligence.

meanings.english: Bought without proper examination; acceptance of something without checking.

content.usage: Buying a used car online is like buying a pig in a poke.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Poke' is a Bag. In old markets, dishonest sellers would put a Cat in a bag but sell it as a 'Pig'. If you didn't check (look in the poke), you got Cheated (ठगा जाना).
extras.origin: Medieval market trickery. 'Poke' means sack/bag. Sellers would substitute a worthless cat for a valuable piglet in a tied bag. Buying it without looking meant making a risky, uninformed purchase.

meanings.english: To be idle; waiting with nothing to do.

content.usage: I was just twiddling my thumbs while waiting for the meeting to start.

extras.mnemonic: Clasp your hands and rotate your Thumbs (अंगूठे) around each other. This useless action is what you do when you are Bored (बोर) and have absolutely nothing to do.
extras.origin: Refers to the physical habit of rotating thumbs around each other when hands are clasped, typically done when bored or idle.

meanings.english: Neighbourhood; specific area.

content.usage: If you are ever in my neck of the woods, come visit me.

extras.mnemonic: A 'Neck' is a narrow strip of land (like a neck connects head to body). In old America, forests were divided into 'necks'. 'My neck of the woods' means My Local Area (मेरा इलाका).
extras.origin: American origin (18th century). 'Neck' referred to a narrow stretch of land or a settlement within a forest. It evolved to mean a specific neighborhood or locality.

meanings.english: To confine somebody in prison; to render powerless.

content.usage: The police finally laid the gang leader by the heels.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: The 'Stocks' (wooden punishment frame). The prisoner's legs were locked in holes by the Heels (एड़ी). Laying someone by the heels means capturing and Imprisoning (कैद करना) them.
extras.origin: Medieval punishment. Refers to putting a prisoner in the stocks or bilboes (iron bars), where their ankles (heels) were shackled, rendering them immobile and powerless.

meanings.english: To leave someone without support or direction.
meanings.hindi: बेसहारा छोड़ देना; मझधार में छोड़ना।

content.usage: After the company closed, the employees felt cast adrift.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Putting a person in a small boat without oars and pushing them into the ocean (Cast). They drift aimlessly. Being 'cast adrift' means being left Helpless (बेसहारा) and directionless.
extras.origin: Nautical term. A boat 'adrift' floats without control of wind or tide. Casting a person adrift (often a punishment for sailors) meant leaving them to the mercy of the sea.

meanings.english: A mood of deep thoughts; reverie.
meanings.hindi: गहन चिंतन; ख्यालों में खोया हुआ।

content.usage: He sat in a brown study, ignoring the chaos around him.

extras.mnemonic: Brown implies gloom/darkness. 'Study' implies deep thought. A 'Brown Study' is a state of being completely Absinthe-minded or Lost in Thought (गहन चिंतन), often ignoring surroundings.
extras.origin: From the French 'brun' (gloomy). Originally meant a state of gloomy or melancholic meditation. Now refers to deep absorption in thought.

meanings.english: Nonsense speech; complicated language that is hard to understand.
meanings.hindi: बकवास; समझ से बाहर की बात।

content.usage: The contract was full of legal mumbo jumbo.

extras.mnemonic: Sounds like tribal chanting. Visual: A masked medicine man shouting words no one understands. 'Mumbo Jumbo' refers to language that sounds confusing, superstitious, or like Nonsense (बकवास).
extras.origin: From 'Mambo Jambo', a masked dancer used by Mandingo tribes in West Africa to settle disputes. European travelers found the rituals confusing and meaningless, hence the term.

meanings.english: To become angry suddenly.

content.usage: Be careful, he flies into a passion over small mistakes.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A bird taking Flight instantly. A person who 'Flies into a Passion' moves from calm to Angry (गुस्सा) in a split second, like a bird taking off.
extras.origin: Descriptive idiom. 'Flying' suggests sudden, rapid movement. 'Passion' here refers specifically to the emotion of anger or rage.

meanings.english: Easily & quickly achievable; 'and there you have it'.

content.usage: Just press this button, and Bob's your uncle!

extras.mnemonic: Story: A Prime Minister named Bob gave a top job to his nephew Arthur. It was so easy for Arthur! So, if 'Bob is your Uncle', Success is Easy (आसान). It means 'Task Done'.
extras.origin: British slang (1887). Prime Minister Robert (Bob) Cecil appointed his nephew Arthur Balfour to a high post. It became a catchphrase for getting something easily through privilege or simplicity.

meanings.english: Incomprehensible; hard to understand.
meanings.hindi: समझ से परे (काला अक्षर भैंस बराबर)।

content.usage: The technical manual was all Latin and Greek to me.

extras.mnemonic: Latin and Greek are ancient, dead languages. If someone speaks them to you, you understand Nothing (कुछ नहीं). It means the topic is too Difficult/Foreign (कठिन) to understand.
extras.origin: Similar to Shakespeare's 'It was Greek to me' (Julius Caesar). It refers to the difficulty of classical languages for the common person, symbolizing incomprehensibility.

meanings.english: Cowards.

content.usage: Don't be such a yellow belly; jump in the water!

extras.mnemonic: Yellow is the color of Cowardice (कायरता). Lizards or frogs with yellow bellies hide in the mud. Calling someone a 'Yellow Belly' calls them a Coward who is afraid to fight.
extras.origin: Uncertain origin. Possibly from yellow-bellied lizards/frogs or the sickness (jaundice) associated with weakness. By the 19th century, 'yellow' was slang for cowardly.

meanings.english: Gesture of peace.

content.usage: He extended an olive branch to his rival to end the feud.

extras.mnemonic: Bible Story: After the Great Flood, a dove returned to Noah's Ark holding an Olive Branch (जैतून की शाखा). It meant the storm was over and Peace (शांति) had returned to earth.
extras.origin: Biblical (Genesis). The dove bringing an olive leaf to Noah showed that the floodwaters had receded and God's anger had ceased. It became a universal symbol of peace.

meanings.english: Failing completely; wasted.
meanings.hindi: बर्बाद हो जाना; नाली में बह जाना।

content.usage: All our hard work went down the tubes when the project was cancelled.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a Toilet or Drain pipe (Tube). If you drop your ring in the sink, it goes 'Down the Tubes' and is lost forever. It means something is Wasted (बर्बाद) or ruined.
extras.origin: Plumbing metaphor. 'Tubes' refers to drainpipes or sewer pipes. Anything that goes down them is lost, flushed away, or ruined beyond recovery.

meanings.english: Go insane; to lose one's mind.

content.usage: Have you lost your marbles? You can't quit your job without a plan!

extras.mnemonic: Marbles (कंचे) represent your Brains/Wits. If you drop your bag of marbles, you have nothing left to play with. 'Losing your marbles' means Going Crazy (पागल होना).
extras.origin: Late 19th-century slang. 'Marbles' was slang for 'wits' or common sense. Losing them implied losing one's mental faculties.

meanings.english: Went terribly wrong.

content.usage: The plan went pear-shaped when it started raining.

extras.mnemonic: A perfect circle is good. A Pear (नाशपाती) is lopsided and fat at the bottom. If a plan 'goes pear-shaped', it loses its perfect form and goes Wrong (गड़बड़).
extras.origin: RAF (Royal Air Force) slang from the 1940s. Originally described a loop maneuver that failed—instead of a perfect circle, the pilot flew a messy 'pear shape'. Generalized to mean any plan going wrong.

meanings.english: A place having many interesting or valuable objects.

content.usage: The antique shop was a veritable Aladdin's cave.

extras.mnemonic: Story: Aladdin found a secret Cave (गुफा) filled with gold, jewels, and magic lamps. Any place filled with Treasures (खजाना) or wonderful things is called 'Aladdin's Cave'.
extras.origin: From 'The Arabian Nights' (One Thousand and One Nights). The story of Aladdin discovering a magical cave filled with riches.

meanings.english: A painful cramp in the leg muscles.

content.usage: I got a bad charley horse while running the marathon.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine being kicked by a Horse named Charley. It hurts your leg! A 'Charley Horse' is a sudden, painful Cramp (ऐंठन) in the leg muscle.
extras.origin: American baseball slang (1880s). Origin debated; possibly named after a lame horse named Charley, or a pitcher named Charley who suffered cramps. It refers to a muscle cramp/stiffness.

meanings.english: Out of one's senses (usually with grief or anger).

content.usage: She was beside herself with grief when she heard the news.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine experiencing emotion so strong that your Soul steps out of your body and stands 'Beside' (बगल में) you. You are not yourself. It means being Overwhelmed (बेसुध) by emotion.
extras.origin: Ancient Greek/Biblical concept. Intense emotion was thought to separate the soul from the body, so a person was metaphorically standing 'beside' themselves.

content.phrase: At the drop of a dime

meanings.english: Happening fast without hesitation.

content.usage: He is ready to argue at the drop of a dime.

extras.mnemonic: A Dime is a small coin. In old payphones, the moment the dime Dropped, the call connected Instantly (तुरंत). It means happening very fast.
extras.origin: American slang. Variation of 'at the drop of a hat'. Also possibly relates to the speed of connecting a call once the coin (dime) drops into the payphone slot.

meanings.english: In fashion; popular.

content.usage: Retro clothes are back in vogue this summer.

extras.mnemonic: Think of the famous fashion magazine 'Vogue'. If something is 'In Vogue', it is Trendy (फैशनेबल) and stylish right now.
extras.origin: From the French word 'vogue' meaning 'wave' or 'course' (as in sailing). It came to mean the prevailing fashion or popular acceptance.

meanings.english: A difficult situation; stirring up trouble.

content.usage: His controversial speech stirred up a hornet's nest.

extras.mnemonic: A Hornet is a large, angry wasp. If you poke their Nest (छत्ता), hundreds will attack you. Stirring a 'hornet's nest' means causing a huge Trouble (मुसीबत) or angry reaction.
extras.origin: Nature metaphor. Hornets are notoriously aggressive when their nest is disturbed. The phrase describes creating a situation of angry, buzzing opposition.

meanings.english: Small steps; an affected or unnatural way of walking.
meanings.hindi: ठुमक कर चलना; नजाकत से चलना।

content.usage: She walked with a mincing gait that annoyed him.

extras.mnemonic: To 'Mince' means to cut into tiny pieces. A 'Mincing Walk' is walking with tiny, short steps (Small Pieces/छोटे कदम), often to look delicate or fancy.
extras.origin: From the verb 'mince', meaning to cut small. It describes a gait consisting of short, affected steps, often implying artificiality or pretension.

meanings.english: A person who thinks himself of high class; snobbish.

content.usage: The villagers found the new neighbors to be rather toffee-nosed.

extras.mnemonic: Imagine a Snob looking down their nose at you. Their nose is stuck up in the air, perhaps stiff like Toffee (टॉफी). It means being Arrogant (घमंडी) and thinking you are better than others.
extras.origin: British slang (late 19th/early 20th century). Possible origins: a nose pointing upwards as if detaching from a bad smell, or 'toffee' representing the rich/upper class.

meanings.english: To feel extremely ashamed or embarrassed.

content.usage: I was so embarrassed I just wanted to curl up and die.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A bug curling into a ball when touched. When you are extremely Embarrassed (शर्मिंदा), you want to make yourself small (Curl Up) and Disappear (Die).
extras.origin: Hyperbolic expression describing the physical urge to retreat into a fetal position and cease existing due to overwhelming shame or mortification.

meanings.english: To perfection; dressed very fashionably.

content.usage: She was dressed to the nines for the gala dinner.

extras.mnemonic: 9 is the highest single digit. If you are dressed 'To the Nines', you are dressed to the Highest Standard (सर्वोत्तम). You look Perfect (परफेक्ट).
extras.origin: Uncertain origin. Theories include: references to the 9 Muses (perfection), 99.9% purity of gold, or a corruption of 'to the eyne' (to the eyes). It signifies the highest degree of quality.

meanings.english: Event that is likely to happen soon.

content.usage: There are big changes on the horizon for our company.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: Looking out at the sea. You see a ship on the Horizon (क्षितिज). It hasn't arrived yet, but it is Coming (आ रहा है). It means something is about to happen in the Future.
extras.origin: Nautical/Visual metaphor. Objects on the horizon are visible and approaching. Figuratively, it refers to events that are foreseeable and approaching in time.

meanings.english: Small part of a big organization; someone with a minor role.

content.usage: He felt like just a cog in the machine at the giant corporation.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A giant machine with thousands of gears. One tiny tooth (Cog/पुर्जा) is necessary but Unimportant (महत्वहीन) on its own. It represents a worker with no individual power in a big company.
extras.origin: Industrial Revolution metaphor. Workers in factories felt like mechanical parts (cogs) of a vast industrial machine, emphasizing insignificance and interchangeability.

meanings.english: No longer young.

content.usage: At 60, he is no spring chicken, but he still runs marathons.

extras.mnemonic: Chickens born in Spring are young and tender. Old chickens are tough. Saying someone is 'No Spring Chicken' is a polite way of saying they are Old (बूढ़ा).
extras.origin: Agricultural term (18th century). Farmers sold 'spring chickens' (young birds) for higher prices. An older bird trying to pass as young was 'no spring chicken'. Used for people trying to appear younger than they are.

meanings.english: Hard work; manual labour.

content.usage: He built this house by the sweat of his brow.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: A farmer working in the hot sun. Sweat (पसीना) drips from his Brow (Forehead/माथा). It symbolizes Hard Physical Labor (कड़ी मेहनत).
extras.origin: Biblical (Genesis 3:19). God tells Adam, 'In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread', meaning he must work hard for his survival after being expelled from Eden.

meanings.english: At the critical moment; at the time of decision.
meanings.hindi: मुश्किल घड़ी आने पर; निर्णायक समय पर।

content.usage: He promised to help, but when the crunch came, he disappeared.

extras.mnemonic: Think of a machine that Crunches (kuchalna) rocks. When the pressure is maximum, the rock breaks. 'The Crunch' is the Critical Moment (निर्णायक पल) of extreme pressure or decision.
extras.origin: Possibly related to 'Credit Crunch' or mechanical pressure. It refers to a critical point where a decision is forced by pressure or crisis.

meanings.english: To perform without rehearsals; to decide what to do as the situation develops.
meanings.hindi: मौके के हिसाब से काम करना (बिना पूर्व योजना के)।

content.usage: We don't have a plan for the weekend yet; let's just play it by ear.

extras.mnemonic: Musicians usually read sheet music. If they 'Play by Ear', they listen and improvise without a script. It means acting Without a Plan (बिना योजना के) based on the situation.
extras.origin: Musical terminology. Musicians who can play a tune after hearing it, without needing written notes, play 'by ear'. It implies improvisation.

meanings.english: Poor memory; forgets things easily.

content.usage: I need to write everything down because I have a memory like a sieve.

extras.mnemonic: A Sieve (छलनी) has holes to let water drain out. If your brain is a sieve, Information (जानकारी) drains out immediately. You Forget (भूलना) everything.
extras.origin: Kitchen utensil metaphor. A sieve retains nothing liquid. A mind compared to a sieve retains no information.

meanings.english: Strange person.

content.usage: He stays in his room all day and talks to himself; he's a bit of a queer fish.

extras.mnemonic: A Fish that swims backward or has three eyes is Weird. 'Queer' means Strange (अजीब). Calling someone a 'Queer Fish' means they are Odd or Eccentric.
extras.origin: British colloquialism. 'Queer' originally meant strange or odd. 'Fish' referred to a person (like 'cold fish'). Combined, it denotes an eccentric character.

meanings.english: To make an attempt.

content.usage: I've never played golf before, but I'll have a bash.

extras.mnemonic: To 'Bash' means to hit hard. If you 'Have a Bash', you take a swing at something to see if you can do it. It means giving it a Try (कोशिश करना).
extras.origin: British informal slang. 'Bash' implies a strike or attempt. To 'have a bash' is to make a spirited attempt at something.

meanings.english: Spoil someone's chance of doing something.

content.usage: He queered the pitch for me by telling the boss about my mistake.

extras.mnemonic: In Cricket, the Pitch (मैदान) must be smooth. If someone damages or 'Queers' (spoils) the pitch, the game is Ruined (बर्बाद). It means secretly sabotaging a plan.
extras.origin: 19th Century slang. Likely from market vendors; a 'pitch' was their sales spot. If someone spoiled their business spot, they 'queered the pitch'. Alternatively from sport (cricket) where a damaged pitch ruins the game.

meanings.english: Great confusion; in a disordered manner.

content.usage: The children ran pell-mell out of the school when the bell rang.

extras.mnemonic: Sounds like a chaotic rhyme. Imagine people running and falling over each other. 'Pell Mell' describes a Chaotic (अस्त-व्यस्त) and hurried rush.
extras.origin: From the French 'pêle-mêle' (mixed up). It describes a disorderly, confused crowd or rushing movement.

meanings.english: Not brave; cowardly.

content.usage: He was too lily-livered to stand up to the bully.

extras.mnemonic: In old medicine, the Liver contained blood (Courage). A white Lily (लिली का फूल) is pale. A 'Lily Liver' has no red blood, meaning No Courage (हिम्मत नहीं). It means Cowardly.
extras.origin: Medieval physiology. It was believed that the liver produced blood. A pale or 'white' liver (color of a lily) indicated a lack of blood, and therefore a lack of courage.

meanings.english: Make someone extremely angry or irritated.

content.usage: His constant complaining is driving me up the wall.

extras.mnemonic: Visual: You are stuck in a room with an annoying person. You are so Desperate to escape that you try to Climb the Wall (दीवार चढ़ना). It means being extremely Irritated (परेशान).
extras.origin: Hyperbolic expression of frustration. Similar to 'climbing the walls' from boredom or confinement. It implies being driven to a point of madness where one wants to escape by any means.
